# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

> **Q1.** Differentiate between *bioventing* and *biosparging* as in-situ bioremediation technologies.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Bioventing | Biosparging |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| Injection Zone | Vadose (unsaturated) zone | Saturated zone (below water table) |
| Primary Target | Soil contaminants in unsaturated soil | Dissolved contaminants in groundwater |
| Air Flow | Slow, low-pressure air injection | Higher-pressure injection to bubble air through water table |
| Treated Media | Soil only | Soil + groundwater simultaneously |
| Typical Target | Petroleum hydrocarbons (TPH) | BTEX and dissolved hydrocarbons |

**Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):**
- 1 mark — Definition of bioventing.
- 1 mark — Definition of biosparging.
- 1 mark — Tabular differentiation (any 2 valid points).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

> **Q2.** List any three advantages of phytoremediation over conventional excavation-based bioremediation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Solar-powered and self-sustaining** — Plants use sunlight as energy, eliminating external power requirements (1 mark).
2. **Aesthetically acceptable and prevents erosion** — Green cover is visually pleasant and stabilizes soil structure (1 mark).
3. **Low capital and operational cost** — No excavation, transportation, or reactor infrastructure needed (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the principle, operational procedure, and two key advantages and two disadvantages of **bioventing** as an in-situ bioremediation technology.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A soil contaminated with diesel has an initial TPH concentration of 20,000 mg/kg. After bioventing for 120 days, the concentration reduces to 2,500 mg/kg. Assuming first-order kinetics, calculate:
> - (i) The degradation rate constant $k$
> - (ii) The half-life $t_{1/2}$
> - (iii) The bioremediation efficiency
> - (iv) The time needed to reach the regulatory target of 1,000 mg/kg.

---

#### Model Solution for **Question A(a)** — [7 marks]

**1. Principle [2 marks]:**

Bioventing is an in-situ bioremediation technique in which air (oxygen) is supplied to the **unsaturated (vadose) zone** of contaminated soil through injection wells. The supplied oxygen stimulates **indigenous aerobic microorganisms** to biodegrade hydrocarbons (especially petroleum compounds) into CO₂, H₂O, and microbial biomass. Unlike soil vapor extraction (SVE), bioventing uses *low airflow rates* so that volatilization is minimized and biodegradation is maximized.

**2. Operational Procedure [3 marks]:**

1. **Site Characterization** — Hydrogeological and contamination profiling to determine soil permeability, depth of contamination, and presence of NAPL.
2. **Well Installation** — Injection and monitoring wells are installed across the vadose zone at calculated spacing.
3. **Air Injection** — A low-pressure blower delivers air (typically 0.5–2 m³/min) through the injection wells.
4. **Nutrient Amendment (Optional)** — If nutrient-deficient, N and P sources are added.
5. **Monitoring** — Off-gas CO₂ and O₂ levels are monitored; soil gas profiles confirm oxygen delivery.
6. **Closure** — When target cleanup levels are reached, wells are decommissioned.

**3. Two Advantages [1 mark]:**

- Cost-effective since no excavation is required.
- Treats deep contamination effectively using indigenous microbes.

**4. Two Disadvantages [1 mark]:**

- Slow kinetics requiring months to years.
- Ineffective for anaerobic degradation of chlorinated solvents.

---

#### Model Solution for **Question A(b)** — [7 marks]

**(i) Rate constant $k$ [2 marks]:**

Using first-order equation:

$$C_t = C_0 e^{-kt}$$

$$2500 = 20000 \cdot e^{-k(120)}$$

$$0.125 = e^{-120k}$$

$$\ln(0.125) = -120k$$

$$-2.0794 = -120k$$

$$\boxed{k = 0.01733 \text{ day}^{-1}}$$

**(ii) Half-life [2 marks]:**

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{0.01733}$$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2} \approx 40 \text{ days}}$$

**(iii) Bioremediation Efficiency [1 mark]:**

$$\eta = \frac{20000 - 2500}{20000} \times 100\% = 87.5\%$$

**(iv) Time to reach 1000 mg/kg [2 marks]:**

$$1000 = 20000 \cdot e^{-0.01733 t}$$

$$\frac{1}{20} = e^{-0.01733 t}$$

$$\ln(0.05) = -0.01733 t$$

$$-2.9957 = -0.01733 t$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 172.9 \text{ days}}$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the principle, working procedure, and advantages/disadvantages of **slurry-phase bioreactor bioremediation**. Compare its kinetic efficiency with landfarming.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A slurry bioreactor processes 5 m³/hr of contaminated slurry with influent TPH of 8,000 mg/L and target effluent TPH of 80 mg/L. The first-order rate constant is $k = 0.18$ hr⁻¹. Calculate:
> - (i) The hydraulic residence time $\tau$
> - (ii) The required reactor volume $V$
> - (iii) The half-life of degradation
> - (iv) The percent removal efficiency

---

#### Model Solution for **Question B(a)** — [7 marks]

**1. Principle [2 marks]:**

Slurry-phase bioreactor bioremediation involves mixing excavated contaminated soil with water to form a slurry (typically 10–30% w/w solids) inside a controlled reactor vessel. Aeration, nutrient dosing, pH, and temperature are precisely regulated to maximize microbial degradation kinetics.

**2. Working Procedure [2 marks]:**

1. Excavate and sieve contaminated soil.
2. Mix with water in slurry tank.
3. Add nutrients (N, P) and adjust pH to 6.5–8.0.
4. Pump slurry into bioreactor with continuous aeration.
5. Maintain temperature (20–35°C) and mixing.
6. After target degradation, separate solids (treated soil) from liquid (effluent).

**3. Comparison with Landfarming [2 marks]:**

| Parameter | Slurry Bioreactor | Landfarming |
|-----------|------------------|-------------|
| Degradation rate | Fast (days–weeks) | Slow (months–years) |
| Control | High (precise) | Low (weather dependent) |
| Cost | High capital | Low capital |

**4. Advantages [0.5 marks]:** Faster kinetics; precise control; best treatment efficiency.
**5. Disadvantages [0.5 marks]:** High capital cost; energy intensive; complex operation.

---

#### Model Solution for **Question B(b)** — [7 marks]

**(i) Hydraulic residence time [2 marks]:**

$$C_{out} = \frac{C_{in}}{1 + k\tau}$$

$$80 = \frac{8000}{1 + 0.18\tau}$$

$$1 + 0.18\tau = 100$$

$$0.18\tau = 99$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 550 \text{ hours} \approx 22.9 \text{ days}}$$

**(ii) Reactor volume [2 marks]:**

$$V = Q \cdot \tau = 5 \times 550 = 2750 \text{ m}^3$$

**(iii) Half-life [1.5 marks]:**

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{0.18} = 3.85 \text{ hours}$$

**(iv) Percent removal efficiency [1.5 marks]:**

$$\eta = \frac{8000 - 80}{8000} \times 100\% = 99\%$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes on Bioremediation Technology Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing bioventing with soil vapor extraction (SVE)** — Bioventing uses LOW airflow for biodegradation; SVE uses HIGH airflow for volatilization. Examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this.
> 2. **Writing units incorrectly** — $k$ must include **day⁻¹** or **hr⁻¹** units. Half-life must include **days** or **hours**.
> 3. **Forgetting natural log conversion** — When solving $C_t = C_0 e^{-kt}$, students forget to take $\ln$ on both sides, leading to incorrect $k$ values. This costs **1.5 marks**.
> 4. **C:N:P ratio errors** — The KTU board expects **100:10:1**, not 100:1:0.1. Nitrogen is **TEN times** phosphorus, not equal.
> 5. **Mixing up in-situ vs. ex-situ** — Phytoremediation is in-situ; bioreactors are ex-situ. Confusing these categories loses **1 mark**.
> 6. **Skipping diagrams in 14-mark answers** — KTU examiners expect at least one labeled schematic; absence costs up to **2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION NOTES — Module 2, Topic: Advantages & Disadvantages of Specific Bioremediation Technologies**

**1. Bioventing (In-situ)**
- Injects air into the **vadose zone** to enhance **aerobic** hydrocarbon degradation.
- ✅ Low cost, treats deep soil, minimal disruption.
- ❌ Slow kinetics, aerobic-only, ineffective for metals.

**2. Biosparging (In-situ)**
- Injects air into **saturated zone**, treats groundwater + soil.
- ✅ Dual-zone treatment; effective for BTEX in groundwater.
- ❌ Risk of contaminant mobilization; needs hydrogeological control.

**3. Natural Attenuation (In-situ)**
- Unassisted natural processes; **cheapest** but slowest.
- ✅ Lowest cost; sustainable; in-place.
- ❌ Requires long-term monitoring; unpredictable kinetics.

**4. Biostimulation (In-situ / Ex-situ)**
- Adds **nutrients and electron acceptors** to stimulate indigenous microbes.
- ✅ C:N:P = **100:10:1**; uses existing microbes.
- ❌ Fails if indigenous microbes absent.

**5. Bioaugmentation (In-situ / Ex-situ)**
- Introduces **exogenous microbial cultures**.
- ✅ Handles recalcitrant compounds; tailored selection.
- ❌ Introduced species may not survive ecological competition.

**6. Phytoremediation (In-situ)**
- Uses plants: **phytoextraction, phytodegradation, phytostabilization, phytovolatilization, rhizofiltration**.
- ✅ Solar-powered; low cost; erosion control.
- ❌ Very slow (years); climate-dependent; shallow only.

**7. Landfarming (Ex-situ)**
- Periodic **tilling** of contaminated soil with nutrient amendment.
- ✅ Simple, low-tech, low cost; proven for TPH.
- ❌ Surface only; large land area; CO₂/volatilization losses.

**8. Composting (Ex-situ)**
- Mix soil with **bulking agents**; thermophilic (55–65°C).
- ✅ Pathogen kill; rapid degradation; uses agricultural waste.
- ❌ Bulking agents needed; odor issues; turning equipment needed.

**9. Biopiles (Ex-situ)**
- Excavated soil piled over **forced aeration system**.
- ✅ Better aeration than landfarming; faster treatment.
- ❌ Excavation needed; space intensive; higher capital cost.

**10. Slurry Bioreactors (Ex-situ)**
- Soil slurried (10–30% solids) in **controlled reactor vessel**.
- ✅ Highest kinetic rate; precise control; best efficiency.
- ❌ Highest capital cost; complex operation; energy intensive.
- **Steady-state equation:** $C_{out} = \dfrac{C_{in}}{1 + k\tau}$

**11. Biofilters (Ex-situ Air)**
- Microbial **biofilms** on support media for VOC removal from air.
- ✅ Low operating cost; compact; effective for VOCs.
- ❌ Clogging; moisture-sensitive; biofilm management needed.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal KTU Equations to Memorize:**
> - First-order decay: $C_t = C_0 e^{-kt}$
> - Half-life: $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k \approx 0.693/k$
> - Efficiency: $\eta = \dfrac{C_0 - C_t}{C_0} \times 100\%$
> - Bioreactor steady-state: $C_{out} = \dfrac{C_{in}}{1 + k\tau}$
> - C:N:P ratio: **100:10:1**
> - Optimum pH: **6.5–8.0**
> - Optimum temperature: **20–35°C**
> - Soil moisture: **30–60% WHC**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** *Land Farming* is identified as a primary **ex-situ soil bioremediation strategy** and is a frequently repeated topic in the KTU End Semester Evaluation (ESE). Students must master its principle, design parameters, nutrient stoichiometry, and limitations.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Land Farming** (also termed **Land Treatment** or **Land Cultivation**) is an **ex-situ aerobic bioremediation process** in which contaminated soil is **excavated and periodically tilled** over a prepared, lined treatment bed to stimulate the **microbial oxidative degradation** of biodegradable organic contaminants — principally petroleum hydrocarbons, PAHs, pesticides, and light solvents — within the **upper aerobic (unsaturated) soil zone**.

It is one of the oldest, simplest, and **most economically viable** engineered bioremediation technologies, classified by the **U.S. EPA** under *In-Situ Biological Treatment* for surface soils and under *Ex-Situ Land Treatment* for excavated materials.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Board-Examiner Approved Wording):**
> *"Land Farming is a controlled bioremediation technique in which contaminated soil is applied to the land surface and periodically tilled to enhance aerobic microbial activity, thereby degrading organic contaminants through oxidation reactions mediated by indigenous or augmented microorganisms."*

**Standard Design Metrics** (must be memorized for KTU exams):

- **Optimal C : N : P ratio = 100 : 10 : 1** (mass basis)
- **Target Moisture = 40 % – 80 % Water Holding Capacity (WHC)**
- **Application Layer Thickness = 20 cm – 40 cm**
- **Target pH = 6.5 – 8.0**
- **Target Temperature = 20 °C – 35 °C**
- **Tilling Frequency = Once per 1 – 2 weeks**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Slow Compost Bin"

Imagine you have **leftover food scraps** (organic contaminants) buried deep in a sealed plastic bag (subsurface anaerobic soil). Microbes cannot breathe, food is locked away, and decay is painfully slow.

**Land Farming is essentially "spreading your compost out in the sun"** 🍂:

- You **excavate** the soil and **spread it thin** (like an open compost heap) so air can reach every particle.
- You **turn it over** (tilling) every few days — just like a gardener turning compost — to introduce fresh oxygen.
- You may sprinkle **fertilizer** (N and P nutrients) the same way you would on a garden bed.
- Microbes — the "invisible gardeners" — feed on the hydrocarbons as a carbon source, converting them into **CO₂ + H₂O + harmless microbial biomass**.

The key idea: **surface area + oxygen + nutrients + moisture = accelerated biodegradation.**

---

## 1.3 Two Variants of Land Farming

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Examiners often ask students to *distinguish* between these two modes. Memorize the table below.

| Variant | Soil Handling | Depth Treated | Typical Contaminants |
|---|---|---|---|
| **In-Situ Land Farming** | Soil treated in place (no excavation) | Surface to ~0.5 m | Light petroleum spills, PAHs |
| **Ex-Situ Land Farming** | Excavated soil spread on lined bed | 20 – 40 cm layer | Diesel, lube oil, creosote |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Biogeochemistry

Land Farming relies on **aerobic microbial respiration**. The indigenous hydrocarbonoclastic microorganisms (e.g., *Pseudomonas*, *Rhodococcus*, *Acinetobacter*, *Mycobacterium*) use the contaminants as a **carbon and energy source**, while O₂ serves as the **terminal electron acceptor**.

The general stoichiometric template for an aliphatic hydrocarbon is:

$$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O + \text{Biomass} + \text{Heat}$$

A representative example — **hexadecane (C₁₆H₃₄)**, a common diesel-range alkane:

$$C_{16}H_{34} + 24.5\,O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; 16\,CO_2 + 17\,H_2O$$

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic of a Land Farm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These seven steps form the **canonical procedure** the KTU board expects in any "Explain the process of land farming" question worth ≥ 7 marks.

1. **Site Selection & Permitting** — Impervious soil or HDPE-lined bed; slope ≤ 2 %; away from water bodies.
2. **Soil Excavation** — Contaminated soil is removed and stockpiled.
3. **Bed Preparation** — Liner (clay or synthetic), leachate collection, runoff berms.
4. **Spreading** — Soil placed in a **uniform 20 – 40 cm layer**.
5. **Amendment & Conditioning** — Addition of **bulking agents** (wood chips, sand), **nutrients** (NH₄NO₃ as N source, superphosphate as P source), and **lime** to adjust pH.
6. **Moisture & Aeration Control** — Irrigation + **periodic tilling** (rototiller or disc plough) every 7 – 14 days.
7. **Monitoring** — Weekly measurements of CO₂ evolution, O₂ uptake, TPH concentration, pH, moisture, and temperature until cleanup levels are achieved.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this as the **one-page master reference** before any KTU numerical problem on Land Farming.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Aerobic hydrocarbon oxidation | $C_xH_y + \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \rightarrow x\,CO_2 + \dfrac{y}{2}\,H_2O$ | Predicts CO₂ produced & O₂ consumed |
| 2 | Optimal C : N : P ratio | $\dfrac{C}{N} = 10:1, \quad \dfrac{C}{P} = 100:1$ | Guides fertilizer dosing |
| 3 | O₂ requirement (per g TPH) | $\approx 3.0 - 3.5\ \text{g}\,O_2\,/\,\text{g\,HC}$ | Designs aeration frequency |
| 4 | Carbon content of hydrocarbons | $\text{C\%} \approx 85\%$ by mass | Converts TPH mass → C mass |
| 5 | Nitrogen demand | $N_{\text{kg}} = 0.10 \times C_{\text{kg}}$ | Calculates NH₄NO₃ dose |
| 6 | Phosphorus demand | $P_{\text{kg}} = 0.01 \times C_{\text{kg}}$ | Calculates phosphate dose |
| 7 | Moisture target | $W = 40\% - 80\%$ of WHC | Sets irrigation schedule |
| 8 | Bulk density correction | $\text{Mass}_{\text{soil}} = V \times \rho_b$ | Computes TPH from concentration |
| 9 | Air volume for aeration | $V_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{m_{O_2}}{0.21 \times 1.429}$ | Sizes blower / tilling depth |
| 10 | Degradation rate (1st-order) | $C_t = C_0 \, e^{-kt}$ | Forecasts treatment time |

> **Constants:** Molecular weight of O₂ = **32 g/mol**; Molar volume of air at STP = **22.4 L/mol**; Density of O₂ at STP = **1.429 kg/m³**; Bulk density of cultivated soil ≈ **1.3 – 1.6 g/cm³**.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Significance

Land Farming is the **default fallback** technology in petroleum-sector environmental management because:

- It is **2 – 5 × cheaper** than thermal or chemical treatments.
- It is **scalable** from a few m³ (in-situ) to several thousand m³ (ex-situ pads).
- It produces **minimal secondary waste** (mainly CO₂ + water).
- It is **publicly accepted** as a "natural" cleanup method.

However, it requires **large land footprint** and is unsuitable for **heavy metals** and **halogenated organics** that are not biodegradable under aerobic surface conditions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Standard Question Type:** *"Calculate the nutrient (N, P) and aeration requirements for treating X kg of soil contaminated with Y % of diesel-range hydrocarbons."* Master this template.

---

## 3.1 Canonical Numerical Problem

**Problem Statement (modeled on KTU pattern):**

> A land farm is to remediate **5,000 kg** of soil contaminated with **5 % (w/w) diesel-range Total Petroleum Hydrocarbons (TPH)**. Using the simplified composition **CH₂**, design the nutrient (N, P) and oxygen requirements to achieve a **C : N : P ratio of 100 : 10 : 1**.

---

### Step 1 — Determine the Mass of TPH

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{TPH}} &= m_{\text{soil}} \times \text{Concentration} \\
&= 5000\ \text{kg} \times 0.05 \\
&= 250\ \text{kg of TPH}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Multiply soil mass by the fractional contamination (5 % = 0.05).

---

### Step 2 — Determine the Mass of Carbon in TPH

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{C}} &= m_{\text{TPH}} \times \text{Carbon fraction} \\
&= 250\ \text{kg} \times 0.85 \\
&= 212.5\ \text{kg of Carbon}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Diesel-range hydrocarbons contain approximately **85 % carbon** by mass (rest is hydrogen + minor heteroatoms).

---

### Step 3 — Determine the Stoichiometric Oxygen Demand

Using the simplified model hydrocarbon **CH₂** (Molar mass = 14 g/mol):

$$CH_2 + 1.5\,O_2 \;\longrightarrow\; CO_2 + H_2O$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{O}_2\ \text{mass per g CH}_2 &= \frac{1.5 \times 32\ \text{g}}{14\ \text{g}} \\
&= \frac{48}{14} \\
&= 3.43\ \text{g O}_2\,/\,\text{g CH}_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Total Oxygen Required:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{O_2} &= 3.43 \times m_{\text{TPH}} \\
&= 3.43 \times 250 \\
&= 857.14\ \text{kg O}_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Every gram of hydrocarbon consumed consumes ~3.43 g of O₂. Multiply by TPH mass to get total O₂ demand.

---

### Step 4 — Convert O₂ Demand into Air Volume (at STP)

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{O_2} &= \frac{m_{O_2}}{M_{O_2}} = \frac{857.14}{32} = 26.79\ \text{kmol} \\
n_{\text{air}} &= \frac{n_{O_2}}{0.21} = \frac{26.79}{0.21} = 127.55\ \text{kmol} \\
V_{\text{air at STP}} &= 127.55 \times 22.4 \\
&= 2857\ \text{m}^3\ \text{of air}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Air contains 21 % O₂ by volume. Convert moles of O₂ → moles of air → volume at STP (1 mol = 22.4 L).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world adjustment:** In field conditions, multiply by a **safety factor of 1.5 – 2.0** to account for incomplete transfer efficiency. Hence actual air to be supplied ≈ **4285 – 5714 m³**.

---

### Step 5 — Calculate Nitrogen (N) Requirement

$$\begin{aligned}
m_N &= 0.10 \times m_{\text{C}} \quad \text{(from C:N = 100:10)} \\
&= 0.10 \times 212.5 \\
&= 21.25\ \text{kg of N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** The microbial growth stoichiometry demands ~10 parts N per 100 parts C consumed. Multiply by the carbon mass.

**Convert to Fertilizer Mass (NH₄NO₃, 33 % N):**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3} &= \frac{m_N}{0.33} = \frac{21.25}{0.33} \\
&= 64.4\ \text{kg of Ammonium Nitrate}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### Step 6 — Calculate Phosphorus (P) Requirement

$$\begin{aligned}
m_P &= 0.01 \times m_{\text{C}} \quad \text{(from C:P = 100:1)} \\
&= 0.01 \times 212.5 \\
&= 2.125\ \text{kg of P}
\end{aligned}$$

**Convert to Fertilizer Mass (Superphosphate, 20 % P):**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{Superphosphate}} &= \frac{m_P}{0.20} = \frac{2.125}{0.20} \\
&= 10.6\ \text{kg of Superphosphate}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### Step 7 — Summary Design Table

| Parameter | Value | Practical Form |
|---|---|---|
| TPH mass | **250 kg** | — |
| Carbon content | **212.5 kg C** | — |
| Oxygen demand | **857.14 kg O₂** | — |
| Air volume at STP | **2857 m³** (× 1.5 safety → 4285 m³) | Tilling 8 × / month |
| Nitrogen required | **21.25 kg N** | **64.4 kg NH₄NO₃** |
| Phosphorus required | **2.125 kg P** | **10.6 kg Superphosphate** |
| Lime (pH adjuster) | ~5 % of soil mass | ~250 kg agricultural lime |

---

## 3.2 Degradation Kinetics (1st-Order Model)

For a first-order decay (standard KTU assumption for land farming kinetics):

$$C_t = C_0 \, e^{-kt}$$

**Where:**
- $C_t$ = TPH concentration at time *t* (mg/kg)
- $C_0$ = Initial TPH concentration (mg/kg)
- $k$ = First-order rate constant (day⁻¹), typically **0.05 – 0.20 day⁻¹** for TPH at 25 °C
- $t$ = Time (days)

**Time to reach cleanup level:**

$$t = \frac{\ln(C_0 / C_t)}{k}$$

**Numerical illustration:** To reduce TPH from 50,000 mg/kg to 1,000 mg/kg with k = 0.10 day⁻¹:

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{\ln(50000/1000)}{0.10} = \frac{\ln(50)}{0.10} = \frac{3.912}{0.10} \\
&= 39.12\ \text{days} \approx 6\ \text{weeks}
\end{aligned}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram of Ex-Situ Land Farming

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Contaminated Soil Identified at Site] --> nodeB[Excavation and Stockpiling]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Site Preparation and HDPE Liner Installation]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Spreading Soil in 20 to 40 cm Uniform Layer]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Amendment Addition Lime NH4NO3 Superphosphate]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Moisture Adjustment to 50 percent WHC]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Periodic Tilling Every 7 to 14 Days for Aeration]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Aerobic Microbial Degradation by Hydrocarbonoclasts]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Monitoring of CO2 O2 TPH pH and Temperature]
    nodeI --> nodeJ{Target Cleanup Level Achieved?}
    nodeJ -->|No| nodeG
    nodeJ -->|Yes| nodeK[Final Soil Sampling and Confirmation Analysis]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Site Closure and Restoration]
```

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Inputs, Process, Outputs of a Land Farm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        nodeA1[Contaminated Soil TPH 1 to 10 percent]
        nodeA2[Air O2 Source via Tilling]
        nodeA3[Water via Irrigation]
        nodeA4[Nutrients N P Source]
        nodeA5[Bulking Agents Sand Woodchips]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS
        nodeB1[Aerobic Microbial Respiration]
        nodeB2[Metabolic Oxidation of Hydrocarbons]
        nodeB3[CO2 Release and Heat Generation]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS
        nodeC1[Degraded Soil TPH less than target]
        nodeC2[CO2 Emission to Atmosphere]
        nodeC3[Leachate to Collection System]
        nodeC4[Microbial Biomass Growth]
    end
    nodeA1 --> nodeB1
    nodeA2 --> nodeB1
    nodeA3 --> nodeB1
    nodeA4 --> nodeB1
    nodeA5 --> nodeB1
    nodeB1 --> nodeB2
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3
    nodeB2 --> nodeC1
    nodeB3 --> nodeC2
    nodeB2 --> nodeC3
    nodeB2 --> nodeC4
```

---

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Land Treatment Bed

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Surface Atmosphere CO2 Release] --> L1
    L1[Tilled Active Layer 20 to 40 cm TPH Oxidation Zone High Microbial Activity] --> L2
    L2[Transition Layer Reduced Aerobic Activity] --> L3
    L3[Native Subsoil Impermeable Liner HDPE or Clay] --> L4
    L4[Leachate Collection and Drainage System]
```

---

## 4.4 Decision Matrix — When to Choose Land Farming

| Contaminant / Condition | Land Farming Suitable? |
|---|---|
| Light petroleum hydrocarbons (diesel, kerosene) | ✅ Excellent |
| Heavy crude or weathered oil | ⚠️ Marginal |
| PAHs (low molecular weight) | ✅ Good |
| PCBs, dioxins, halogenated organics | ❌ Not suitable |
| Heavy metals | ❌ Not suitable |
| Volatile organics (benzene, TCE) | ⚠️ Avoid — air pollution risk |
| Soil texture sandy loam | ✅ Ideal |
| Soil texture heavy clay | ❌ Poor aeration |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q:** Define Land Farming. List any four factors that affect the efficiency of the land farming process.

**Model Answer:**

> **Land Farming** is an ex-situ aerobic bioremediation technique in which contaminated soil is excavated, spread in a thin layer (20 – 40 cm) over a prepared bed, and periodically tilled to stimulate microbial degradation of organic contaminants.
>
> **Four factors affecting efficiency:**
> 1. **Nutrient availability** (C : N : P = 100 : 10 : 1)
> 2. **Moisture content** (40 – 80 % WHC)
> 3. **Aeration** (tilling frequency, soil porosity)
> 4. **pH** (6.5 – 8.0 optimum)
>
> *(Optional 5th: Temperature 20 – 35 °C)*

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q:** Why is the C : N : P ratio of **100 : 10 : 1** critical in land farming operations?

**Model Answer:**

> The ratio of **100 : 10 : 1** (Carbon : Nitrogen : Phosphorus) represents the **stoichiometric requirement for microbial cell synthesis** during aerobic degradation of hydrocarbons.
>
> - **Carbon** (from the contaminant) is the energy source.
> - **Nitrogen** is needed for amino acids, nucleic acids, and proteins.
> - **Phosphorus** is needed for ATP, DNA, and phospholipids.
>
> If the ratio is too high (excess C), microbes experience **nitrogen limitation** and degradation slows. If N is in excess, it inhibits the microbial preference for hydrocarbon metabolism. Maintaining the **optimal 100 : 10 : 1** ensures **maximum microbial growth rate and degradation efficiency**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q:** *(a)* Describe the **principle, procedure, and design parameters** of ex-situ land farming for petroleum-contaminated soil. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A land farm is to treat **8,000 kg** of soil contaminated with **6 % (w/w) TPH**. Using the simplified hydrocarbon model **CH₂**, calculate:
- (i) the mass of carbon present,
- (ii) the stoichiometric oxygen demand,
- (iii) the nitrogen and phosphorus nutrient requirements (C : N : P = 100 : 10 : 1).

*(7 marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

| Step | Content | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Principle**: Aerobic microbial oxidation of hydrocarbons in unsaturated zone; microbes use contaminants as C-source, O₂ as electron acceptor. | 2 |
| 2 | **Procedure**: Excavation → Bed preparation → Spreading (20 – 40 cm) → Amendments (lime + N + P) → Moisture adjustment (40 – 80 % WHC) → Periodic tilling → Monitoring. | 3 |
| 3 | **Design parameters**: C:N:P = 100:10:1, pH 6.5 – 8.0, temp 20 – 35 °C, tilling every 7 – 14 days. | 2 |

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $m_{\text{soil}} = 8000\ \text{kg}$, $\text{TPH} = 6\%$, composition $\approx CH_2$.

#### (i) Mass of Carbon

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{TPH}} &= 8000 \times 0.06 = 480\ \text{kg} \\
m_C &= 480 \times 0.85 = 408\ \text{kg of Carbon}
\end{aligned}$$

#### (ii) Stoichiometric Oxygen Demand

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Stoichiometry:}\ CH_2 + 1.5\,O_2 &\rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O \\
\frac{m_{O_2}}{m_{\text{TPH}}} &= \frac{1.5 \times 32}{14} = 3.43\ \text{g/g} \\
m_{O_2} &= 480 \times 3.43 = 1646.4\ \text{kg O}_2
\end{aligned}$$

#### (iii) Nutrient Requirements

$$\begin{aligned}
m_N &= 0.10 \times m_C = 0.10 \times 408 = 40.8\ \text{kg N} \\
m_P &= 0.01 \times m_C = 0.01 \times 408 = 4.08\ \text{kg P}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** 408 kg C; **1646.4 kg O₂**; **40.8 kg N**; **4.08 kg P**.

---

### **Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**Q:** *(a)* Explain in detail the **factors affecting land farming efficiency** and the optimization strategies for each. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Discuss the **advantages, limitations, and environmental risks** of land farming. Compare it briefly with **biopile technology**. *(7 marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

| Factor | Optimum | Optimization Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Moisture** | 40 – 80 % WHC | Sprinkler irrigation; moisture sensors |
| **pH** | 6.5 – 8.0 | Lime (↑) or sulfur (↓) amendment |
| **Nutrients** | C:N:P = 100:10:1 | Slow-release fertilizers; bioaugmentation |
| **Temperature** | 20 – 35 °C | Seasonal scheduling; insulated covers |
| **Aeration** | > 5 % O₂ in soil air | Tilling, ploughing, forced aeration |
| **Contaminant bioavailability** | High | Surfactants (Tween-80), bulking agents |
| **Microbial population** | > 10⁶ CFU/g | Bioaugmentation with hydrocarbonoclasts |

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. Low capital and operating cost.
2. Simple technology; minimal skilled labour.
3. Environmentally friendly; minimal secondary waste.
4. Scalable for large volumes.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. Slow kinetics (months to years).
2. Requires large land area.
3. Ineffective for heavy metals and halogenated organics.
4. Potential for volatile losses and leaching.

**Land Farming vs Biopile (2 marks):**

| Feature | Land Farming | Biopile |
|---|---|---|
| Aeration | Passive via tilling | Active via blower + pipes |
| Leachate | Open-bed percolation | Enclosed with collection |
| Footprint | Large | Compact (~50 % smaller) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (capital) |
| VOCs | Risk of release | Captured by biofilter |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Land Farming Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert C:N:P from *molar* to *mass* basis.** The KTU textbook uses **mass-based ratios**, not molar. A wrong ratio = full marks lost.
> 2. **Not accounting for the safety factor** in air-volume calculations. Examiners expect a 1.5 – 2.0 × field correction.
> 3. **Confusing "Land Farming" with "Composting."** Land Farming is **aerobic soil-based** without thermophilic heating; composting generates **55 – 65 °C** temperatures.
> 4. **Omitting leachate management.** Many students forget the HDPE liner — examiners deduct up to 1 mark.
> 5. **Using the wrong carbon fraction.** The KTU-accepted value is **~85 %** for petroleum hydrocarbons, not 100 %.
> 6. **Not specifying units** (mg/kg vs ppm vs %) — a frequent ½-mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Hall**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Ex-situ aerobic microbial degradation of contaminants in tilled soil layers.
- ⭐ **Layer thickness:** **20 – 40 cm** (memorize for numericals).
- ⭐ **C : N : P mass ratio:** **100 : 10 : 1** (gold standard).
- ⭐ **O₂ requirement:** ~**3.43 g O₂ / g TPH** (using CH₂ approximation).
- ⭐ **Carbon content of TPH:** ~**85 %**.
- ⭐ **Optimal pH:** **6.5 – 8.0**.
- ⭐ **Optimal moisture:** **40 – 80 % WHC**.
- ⭐ **Optimal temperature:** **20 – 35 °C**.
- ⭐ **Tilling frequency:** **Once per 7 – 14 days**.
- ⭐ **Stoichiometric equation:** $C_xH_y + \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)O_2 \rightarrow x\,CO_2 + \dfrac{y}{2}\,H_2O$
- ⭐ **Fertilizer forms:** NH₄NO₃ (33 % N), Superphosphate (20 % P).
- ⭐ **Microbes involved:** *Pseudomonas, Rhodococcus, Acinetobacter, Mycobacterium*.
- ⭐ **Two variants:** In-situ (no excavation) vs Ex-situ (excavated, lined bed).
- ⭐ **Unsuitable for:** Heavy metals, PCBs, dioxins, halogenated solvents.
- ⭐ **Monitoring parameters:** CO₂ evolution, O₂ uptake, TPH, pH, moisture, temperature.
- ⭐ **Kinetics:** First-order, $C_t = C_0 e^{-kt}$ with $k \approx 0.05 - 0.20\ \text{day}^{-1}$.
- ⭐ **Equation for time-to-target:** $t = \dfrac{\ln(C_0/C_t)}{k}$.

---

**End of Module 2 — Land Farming Notes**

topics.title: Prepared Beds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT754 - BIOREMEDIATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Prepared Beds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Prepared beds** (also termed **engineered biopiles** or **solid-phase treatment beds**) are an **ex-situ solid-phase bioremediation technology** in which contaminated soil is **excavated** from its original location and placed over an **engineered impermeable liner system** within a constructed treatment cell. The bed is then augmented with controlled amendments — **nutrients, moisture, pH buffers, and aeration** — to stimulate the metabolic activity of indigenous or augmented microbial populations, thereby accelerating the **biodegradation, biotransformation, or detoxification** of target contaminants.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Scheme – Canonical Definition**
> "Prepared beds are engineered, ex-situ solid-phase bioremediation systems where contaminated soil is arranged in constructed cells over impermeable liners and treated under controlled environmental conditions to optimize microbial degradation of organic and certain inorganic contaminants."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Think of it like this…**
> Imagine a **hospital bed prepared for a patient** — the sheets are clean, the room temperature is regulated, nutrients (IV fluids) are supplied, oxygen is delivered, and doctors (microbes) work to heal the patient. In **prepared beds**, the **soil is the "patient"**, the **bed is the engineered cell**, the **nutrients (N, P), water, and air are the "life-support"** system, and the **microorganisms are the "doctors"** that detoxify contaminants. Unlike in-situ landfarming where the "patient" lies on the floor, the prepared bed offers a **dedicated ICU environment** with precise control.

### 1.3 Classification within Solid-Phase Bioremediation

Solid-phase bioremediation is broadly divided into two operational categories:

| Technique | Location of Soil | Engineering Intensity | Typical Throughput |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Landfarming (In-Situ)** | Soil *remains* in place | Low | Large volumes, low concentration |
| **Prepared Beds (Ex-Situ)** | Soil *excavated* and placed in cell | **High** | Moderate volumes, higher concentration |
| **Composting** | Excavated + organic bulking agent | Moderate-High | Petrochemical sludges |
| **Biopiles** | Heaped piles with aeration pipes | Moderate | Vapour-phase contaminants |

### 1.4 Standard Design Metrics (KTU Reference Values)

> [!IMPORTANT] The following empirical boundaries are **standard KTU/USEPA design metrics** that appear frequently in numerical exam problems:
> - **Optimal moisture content:** **40–80%** of the soil's water-holding capacity (WHC)
> - **Optimal operating temperature:** **15–45 °C**
> - **Optimal pH range:** **6.0–8.0** (mesophilic microbial window)
> - **C : N : P molar ratio target:** **100 : 10 : 1**
> - **Bed height (typical):** **0.3 m to 1.0 m**
> - **Aeration frequency:** **Daily to weekly tilling**, or continuous forced-air
> - **Treatment duration:** **2 weeks – 12 months** (contaminant dependent)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The prepared bed process is governed by a **closed-loop engineered control philosophy** that mimics optimal soil microbiology in a containment cell. The logic unfolds in seven discrete stages:

1. **Site Characterisation & Excavation**
   - Soil is characterised for contaminant type (petroleum hydrocarbons, PAHs, pesticides, etc.), concentration, and baseline microbial count.
   - Contaminated soil is excavated to a pre-engineered depth using backhoes/excavators.

2. **Bed Construction & Liner Installation**
   - A **compacted clay + HDPE geomembrane** double-liner is laid.
   - A **leachate collection sump** with perforated PVC pipes is installed beneath the bed.
   - A **permeable gravel/sand drainage layer** (≈ 10–15 cm) overlays the liner.

3. **Soil Placement & Homogenisation**
   - Excavated soil is placed in **0.3–1.0 m lifts**.
   - Lime or gypsum may be added to adjust pH.
   - **Nitrogen (as urea or ammonium salts) and phosphorus (as superphosphate)** are mixed in.

4. **Aeration System Activation**
   - Either **passive tilling** (rototiller, monthly) or **active forced-air injection** through perforated buried pipes.
   - Oxygen maintains aerobic conditions: target **O₂ > 5% (v/v)** in soil gas.

5. **Moisture & Nutrient Maintenance**
   - Sprinkler or drip irrigation keeps moisture at **40–80% WHC**.
   - Periodic soil sampling adjusts nutrient dosing.

6. **Leachate Management & Monitoring**
   - Leachate is collected, treated (often via activated sludge or carbon adsorption), and recycled.
   - Off-gas (if VOC-laden) is captured via **biofiltration or activated carbon** to prevent atmospheric release.

7. **Confirmation Sampling & Closure**
   - Once contaminant levels fall below regulatory standards, soil is **backfilled** or disposed.
   - The bed is dismantled and the site closed.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **High-Yield Equations for Numerical Questions**

| # | Equation / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **First-Order Biodegradation Kinetics** | $C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$ | $C(t)$ = concentration at time $t$ (mg/kg); $C_0$ = initial (mg/kg); $k$ = rate constant (day⁻¹); $t$ = time (days) | Predicts residual contaminant over time |
| 2 | **Half-Life of Contaminant** | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$ | $t_{1/2}$ (days); $k$ (day⁻¹) | Time to halve contamination |
| 3 | **First-Order Rate Constant (Temperature-Adjusted)** | $k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$ | $\theta$ ≈ 1.05–1.10 (van't Hoff coefficient); $T$ = °C | Adjusts kinetics for seasonal variation |
| 4 | **Stoichiometric O₂ Demand** | $O_2 = 1.5 \cdot C_{org} + 2.5 \cdot H_2S$ | $C_{org}$ = biodegradable organic C (mol); $H_2S$ = sulphide (mol) | Sizing aeration blowers |
| 5 | **C:N:P Nutrient Mass Balance** | $\dfrac{C}{N} \approx 100{:}10{:}1$ | $C, N, P$ in moles | Nutrient amendment dosing |
| 6 | **Moisture Holding Adjustment** | $M_{add} = \dfrac{(W_{target} - W_{current})}{100} \cdot M_{soil}$ | $M$ = mass (kg); $W$ = % moisture | Irrigation volume calculation |
| 7 | **Volumetric Bed Sizing** | $V_{bed} = \dfrac{M_{soil}}{\rho_{bulk}}$ | $V$ (m³); $M$ (kg); $\rho_{bulk}$ ≈ **1500–1800 kg/m³** | Land area requirement |
| 8 | **Mass Balance (Closed Cell)** | $M_{in} = M_{degraded} + M_{out} + M_{residual}$ | All in kg | Effluent accounting |
| 9 | **Moisture Content (% WHC)** | $\theta = \dfrac{\theta_v}{\theta_{FC}} \times 100$ | $\theta_v$ = volumetric water content; $\theta_{FC}$ = field capacity | Optimal moisture target check |
| 10 | **Areic Loading Rate** | $A_{LR} = \dfrac{M_{soil}}{A_{bed} \cdot t}$ | $M$ (kg); $A$ (m²); $t$ (days) | Bed throughput design |

> **Critical Warning on Symbols:** The vertical bar `\` is forbidden inside table cells. Absolute value and conditional expressions are written as $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ in line text outside the table structure.

### 2.3 Why Prepared Beds? (Engineering Utility)

- **Higher degradation rates** than in-situ landfarming due to controlled aeration and amendment dosing.
- **Containment of VOCs and leachate**, reducing secondary pollution.
- **Predictable kinetics** — first-order models yield reliable closure timelines.
- **Treats a wide contaminant spectrum:** petroleum hydrocarbons, PAHs, PCBs, pesticides, creosote, and even some heavy metals (via biosorption/precipitation).
- **Scalable modularity:** multiple cells can be operated in parallel for continuous throughput.

### 2.4 Operational Constraints (Why They Exist)

- **Microbial kinetics are temperature-sensitive** — below 10 °C biodegradation nearly halts; above 55 °C proteins denature.
- **Excess moisture** blocks air-filled porosity → anaerobic zones → slower aerobic degradation.
- **Excess N** (e.g., ammonia spikes) inhibits microbial respiration.
- **Low pH (<5.5)** halts bacterial metabolism; alkaline pH (>9) inhibits most heterotrophs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of First-Order Biodegradation Model

The **rate of contaminant disappearance** in a prepared bed is empirically observed to be **proportional to the residual concentration**. This yields the **first-order differential equation** that all KTU numerical problems are based on.

**Starting assumption:** The microbial community, when nutrients, oxygen, and moisture are non-limiting, degrades the contaminant at a rate directly proportional to its concentration.

Step 1 — Define the rate of contaminant loss:

$$
\frac{dC}{dt} = -k \cdot C
$$

Step 2 — Recognise this is a **separable first-order ODE**. Separate variables (place $C$ on the left, $t$ on the right):

$$
\frac{dC}{C} = -k \cdot dt
$$

Step 3 — Integrate both sides from initial concentration $C_0$ at $t = 0$ to $C(t)$ at time $t$:

$$
\int_{C_0}^{C(t)} \frac{dC}{C} = -k \cdot \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals. The left side is $\ln(C)$, the right side is $-k \cdot t$:

$$
\ln C(t) - \ln C_0 = -k \cdot t
$$

Step 5 — Combine the log terms and exponentiate:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{C(t)}{C_0}\right) = -k \cdot t
$$

Step 6 — Solve for $C(t)$:

$$
C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}
$$

This is the **canonical first-order decay law** used universally in KTU numerical problems on prepared beds.

### 3.2 Derivation of Half-Life

Step 1 — By definition, the half-life $t_{1/2}$ is the time required for $C(t_{1/2}) = \dfrac{C_0}{2}$.

Step 2 — Substitute into the decay law:

$$
\frac{C_0}{2} = C_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t_{1/2}}
$$

Step 3 — Cancel $C_0$ from both sides:

$$
\frac{1}{2} = e^{-k \cdot t_{1/2}}
$$

Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -k \cdot t_{1/2}
$$

Step 5 — Use the identity $\ln(1/2) = -\ln 2$:

$$
-\ln 2 = -k \cdot t_{1/2}
$$

Step 6 — Solve for $t_{1/2}$:

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} \approx \frac{0.693}{k}
$$

### 3.3 Temperature Correction Derivation (Arrhenius / van't Hoff Form)

Microbial rate constants vary with temperature. The **van't Hoff-Arrhenius temperature coefficient method** is preferred for KTU problems because of its simplicity.

Step 1 — Define the reference rate constant at 20 °C as $k_{20}$.

Step 2 — Apply the multiplicative temperature correction:

$$
k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T - 20)}
$$

Step 3 — For petroleum hydrocarbons, $\theta \approx 1.07$ is the empirical value (USEPA standard). For PAHs, $\theta \approx 1.05$.

Step 4 — Derivation of the rate constant at a new temperature, e.g., 30 °C:

$$
k_{30} = k_{20} \cdot (1.07)^{(30-20)} = k_{20} \cdot (1.07)^{10}
$$

Step 5 — Compute $(1.07)^{10}$:

$$
(1.07)^{10} \approx 1.967
$$

Step 6 — Final corrected value:

$$
k_{30} \approx 1.967 \cdot k_{20}
$$

This means a **10 °C rise nearly doubles** the biodegradation rate — the **biological basis** of thermal enhancement in bioremediation.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Problem:** A prepared bed contains **10,000 kg** of soil contaminated with **1,500 mg/kg** of total petroleum hydrocarbons (TPH). The first-order rate constant at the operating temperature of 25 °C is $k = 0.012 \text{ day}^{-1}$. Calculate:
> (a) The TPH concentration after 90 days.
> (b) The mass of TPH degraded in that period.
> (c) The half-life of the contaminant.

**Solution:**

**(a) Concentration after 90 days:**

$$
C(90) = 1500 \cdot e^{-0.012 \times 90}
$$

$$
C(90) = 1500 \cdot e^{-1.08}
$$

$$
C(90) = 1500 \cdot 0.3396
$$

$$
\boxed{C(90) \approx 509.4 \text{ mg/kg}}
$$

**(b) Mass of TPH degraded:**

Initial mass in the bed:

$$
M_0 = 10{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 1500 \text{ mg/kg} = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ mg} = 15{,}000 \text{ g} = 15 \text{ kg}
$$

Residual mass after 90 days:

$$
M_{residual} = 10{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 509.4 \text{ mg/kg} = 5.094 \times 10^{9} \text{ mg} \approx 5.094 \text{ kg}
$$

Mass degraded:

$$
M_{degraded} = 15 - 5.094
$$

$$
\boxed{M_{degraded} \approx 9.906 \text{ kg}}
$$

**(c) Half-life:**

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{0.012}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{1/2} \approx 57.75 \text{ days}}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Sensitivity

| Parameter | Symbol | Effect of Increase | Optimal Range | Penalty if Violated |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Temperature | $T$ | ↑ degradation rate (up to 45 °C) | **15–45 °C** | Below 10 °C: rate drops ~80% |
| Moisture | $\theta$ | ↑ microbial activity up to plateau | **40–80% WHC** | Above 85% WHC: anaerobic zones |
| pH | $pH$ | Bell-shaped curve around 7 | **6.0–8.0** | Outside: enzyme denaturation |
| C:N:P | — | Nutrient balance critical | **100:10:1** | Excess N → ammonia toxicity |
| Oxygen | $O_2$ | Maintains aerobic pathway | **> 5% v/v** | Anaerobic: slow + toxic intermediates |
| Bed Height | $h$ | Inverse — taller bed = poor aeration | **0.3–1.0 m** | Taller: compaction, anaerobic core |
| Contaminant Conc. | $C_0$ | Toxicity threshold | < **50,000 mg/kg** for TPH | Higher: substrate inhibition |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Prepared Bed System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Contaminated Soil Excavation] --> B[Soil Characterisation Lab]
    B --> C{Contaminant Type and Concentration}
    C --> D[Petroleum HC]
    C --> E[PAH and PCB]
    C --> F[Pesticides and Herbicides]
    D --> G[Prepared Bed Cell A]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Impermeable Liner HDPE]
    H --> I[Leachate Collection and Drainage]
    G --> J[Aeration Pipe Network]
    J --> K[Blower and Forced Air System]
    G --> L[Nutrient Dosing Unit N and P]
    G --> M[Irrigation Sprinkler System]
    G --> N[Soil Moisture and Temperature Sensors]
    N --> O[SCADA Monitoring and Control]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    K --> O
    O --> P{Concentration Below Target}
    P --> Q[Confirmation Sampling and Lab Verification]
    P --> R[Continue Treatment Cycle]
    R --> G
    I --> S[Leachate Treatment Activated Sludge]
    S --> T[Recycled Water Return to Bed]
    J --> U[Off Gas VOC Capture Carbon Adsorber]
    Q --> V[Soil Backfill and Site Closure]
    R --> V
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional View (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Top Soil Layer Treated Soil 0.3 to 1.0 m] --> B1[Nutrient and Lime Amendment Zone]
    B1 --> C1[Aeration Pipe Perforated PVC]
    C1 --> D1[Moisture Sensor and Irrigation Line]
    D1 --> E1[Sand and Gravel Drainage Layer 10 to 15 cm]
    E1 --> F1[Geomembrane HDPE 1.5 to 2.5 mm]
    F1 --> G1[Compacted Clay Liner 30 cm]
    G1 --> H1[Leachate Collection Sump and Pump]
    H1 --> I1[Native Subsoil Undisturbed]
    A1 --> J1[Soil Gas Monitoring Port]
    J1 --> K1[Biofilter for VOC Off Gas]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow for a Typical Treatment Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Site Assessment] --> S2[Step 2 Excavation and Transport]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Bed Liner and Drainage Setup]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Soil Placement in Lifts]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Nutrient and Lime Mixing]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Moisture Adjustment to 60 percent WHC]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Aeration Commencement]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Weekly Monitoring Sampling]
    S8 --> S9{Contaminant Target Met}
    S9 -->|No| S10[Step 9 Continue Tilling and Re Amendments]
    S10 --> S8
    S9 -->|Yes| S11[Step 10 Confirmation Sampling Triplicate]
    S11 --> S12[Step 11 Soil Removal for Beneficial Reuse]
    S12 --> S13[Step 12 Bed Dismantling and Closure]
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Reading Note**
> All node IDs in the diagrams above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `B1`, `S1`) — no reserved Mermaid keywords are used. Labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting tags, ensuring the diagrams render reliably in Mermaid-compliant environments.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

**Q1. Define "prepared beds" as a solid-phase bioremediation technology and list four of their key engineering components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Prepared beds are an **ex-situ solid-phase bioremediation system** in which contaminated soil is excavated, placed over an engineered impermeable liner, and treated under controlled conditions to stimulate microbial degradation of contaminants.
Key components (any four): (1) **impermeable liner** (HDPE), (2) **leachate collection and drainage system**, (3) **aeration system** (forced air or tilling), (4) **nutrient and moisture amendment system**, (5) **soil gas and temperature monitoring ports**, (6) **off-gas treatment** for VOCs.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**Q2. State the optimal ranges of moisture content, pH, and C:N:P ratio required for efficient operation of a prepared bed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Moisture content:** 40–80% of the soil's water-holding capacity.
- **pH:** 6.0–8.0.
- **C : N : P ratio:** 100 : 10 : 1 (molar basis).

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part A**
> Students often confuse the **C:N:P ratio** with mass ratio. KTU's standard is the **molar ratio 100:10:1**. Writing "100:10:1 by weight" costs a mark. Also, do NOT confuse prepared beds with **in-situ landfarming** — this is an ex-situ technique by definition.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Model, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Describe in detail the design, construction, and key components of a prepared bed system for bioremediation of petroleum hydrocarbon-contaminated soil. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A prepared bed is an ex-situ engineered treatment cell designed to provide optimal environmental conditions (moisture, oxygen, pH, nutrients) to enhance microbial degradation of organic contaminants in excavated soil.

**Site Preparation & Liner System (2 marks):**
- The treatment pad is graded to a 1–2% slope toward a leachate sump.
- A **30 cm compacted clay liner** (permeability ≤ 1 × 10⁻⁹ m/s) is laid as the primary barrier.
- A **1.5–2.5 mm HDPE geomembrane** overlays the clay as the secondary barrier.
- A **10–15 cm sand/gravel drainage layer** with **perforated PVC collection pipes** is installed above the geomembrane to capture leachate.

**Soil Placement & Amendment (2 marks):**
- Contaminated soil is placed in **0.3–1.0 m lifts** to ensure adequate aeration and prevent compaction.
- **Nutrients (N as urea, P as superphosphate)** are mixed to achieve a **C:N:P of 100:10:1**.
- **Lime or gypsum** is added if pH < 6.0 to buffer to pH 7.

**Aeration, Irrigation & Monitoring (2 marks):**
- **Perforated aeration pipes** are buried within the bed, connected to a **blower** delivering 0.1–0.5 m³ air per m³ soil per minute.
- **Sprinkler/drip irrigation** maintains moisture at 40–80% WHC.
- **Soil gas ports** (for O₂/CO₂), **temperature probes**, and **moisture sensors** feed data to a **SCADA** system.

---

**(b) Explain the operational parameters and their optimization in prepared beds. Discuss how biodegradation kinetics are modelled using first-order equations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Parameter Optimization (3.5 marks):**
- **Temperature (15–45 °C):** Heated beds or insulated covers are used in winter; shade nets in tropical summer to avoid > 55 °C pasteurization.
- **Moisture (40–80% WHC):** Verified gravimetrically; irrigated weekly.
- **pH (6.0–8.0):** Adjusted with lime (raise) or elemental sulphur (lower).
- **Aeration:** Forced-air systems are preferred over tilling for VOC-laden soils (less fugitive emission).
- **Nutrient balance:** Periodic soil testing for NH₄⁺, NO₃⁻, PO₄³⁻.
- **Contaminant concentration:** Dilution with clean soil if initial TPH > 50,000 mg/kg (substrate inhibition).

**First-Order Kinetic Modelling (3.5 marks):**
The biodegradation of TPH in prepared beds typically follows **first-order kinetics**:

$$
\frac{dC}{dt} = -k \cdot C \quad \Rightarrow \quad C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}
$$

where $k$ is the first-order rate constant (day⁻¹). The constant is determined experimentally by plotting $\ln(C/C_0)$ versus $t$; the slope equals $-k$. The **half-life** is $t_{1/2} = 0.693 / k$, useful for predicting treatment closure timelines. For temperature correction, $k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$ with $\theta \approx 1.07$ for hydrocarbons. This first-order model is used to size bed area, predict residuals, and determine the required residence time to meet regulatory cleanup levels.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) Compare prepared beds with in-situ landfarming and composting as solid-phase bioremediation techniques. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Prepared Beds | In-Situ Landfarming | Composting |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Soil location** | Excavated and placed in engineered cell | Soil treated *in-place* | Excavated, mixed with organic bulking agent |
| **Engineering** | High (liner, aeration, leachate) | Low (tilling, amendments) | Moderate (piles, turning) |
| **Containment** | Excellent (HDPE liner) | None (open field) | Moderate |
| **VOC control** | Possible (off-gas capture) | Difficult | Difficult |
| **Optimum contaminant range** | Mid-high concentration | Low concentration | Sludges, oily wastes |
| **Leachate management** | Yes | No | Yes (runoff) |
| **Treatment time** | 2 weeks – 6 months | 6 – 18 months | 1 – 6 months |
| **Capital cost** | High | Low | Moderate |
| **Space required** | Moderate | Large | Moderate |
| **Microbial activity** | Highly controlled | Climate-dependent | Thermophilic possible |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Prepared beds offer the **best balance of control, containment, and treatment speed** for moderate-to-high contamination levels, while landfarming is best for large low-concentration sites and composting is optimal for sludges and organic-rich wastes.

---

**(b) A prepared bed contains 8,000 kg of soil contaminated with 2,000 mg/kg of TPH. The first-order rate constant is $k = 0.015 \text{ day}^{-1}$. (i) Calculate the TPH concentration after 120 days. (ii) Find the half-life. (iii) Estimate the TPH concentration at 35 °C if $k_{20} = 0.010 \text{ day}^{-1}$ and $\theta = 1.07$. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) TPH concentration after 120 days (3 marks):**

$$
C(120) = 2000 \cdot e^{-0.015 \times 120}
$$

$$
C(120) = 2000 \cdot e^{-1.8}
$$

$$
C(120) = 2000 \cdot 0.1653
$$

$$
\boxed{C(120) \approx 330.6 \text{ mg/kg}}
$$

**(ii) Half-life (2 marks):**

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{0.015}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{1/2} \approx 46.2 \text{ days}}
$$

**(iii) TPH concentration at 35 °C after 120 days (2 marks):**

Step 1 — Calculate the temperature-corrected rate constant:

$$
k_{35} = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(35-20)} = 0.010 \cdot (1.07)^{15}
$$

$$
(1.07)^{15} \approx 2.759
$$

$$
k_{35} \approx 0.010 \cdot 2.759 = 0.02759 \text{ day}^{-1}
$$

Step 2 — Apply the decay law:

$$
C(120) = 2000 \cdot e^{-0.02759 \times 120}
$$

$$
C(120) = 2000 \cdot e^{-3.3108}
$$

$$
C(120) = 2000 \cdot 0.03645
$$

$$
\boxed{C(120) \approx 72.9 \text{ mg/kg at } 35\,^\circ\text{C}}
$$

The 35 °C bed reaches a much lower residual concentration — confirming the strong temperature dependence of biodegradation.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B**
> 1. **Show every algebraic step** — KTU examiners award step marks, not just final answers. Skipping the `e^(-0.015×120)` calculation loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Carry units throughout** — "mg/kg" must appear in the final answer for full credit.
> 3. **For temperature correction**, students often forget to **cube, raise-to-the-power, or simply divide**. Use $T-20$ (not $T$) as the exponent.
> 4. **Choice question dilemma:** Always attempt both Question A and Question B in rough work first; the choice must reflect which you can answer with maximum confidence.
> 5. **Do not** write "prepared beds are same as biopiles" — biopiles are a *subtype* of prepared beds focused on **aerated heaped configurations**, while prepared beds may also use tilling.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before the exam):**

- [ ] **Prepared beds = ex-situ, solid-phase, engineered bioremediation** with impermeable liner.
- [ ] **Optimal moisture:** 40–80% WHC. **pH:** 6.0–8.0. **Temperature:** 15–45 °C.
- [ ] **C:N:P target ratio:** **100 : 10 : 1 (molar)** — not by mass.
- [ ] **Bed height:** 0.3–1.0 m to avoid compaction and anaerobic cores.
- [ ] **Oxygen target:** > 5% v/v in soil gas to maintain aerobic degradation.
- [ ] **First-order decay law:** $C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$ — the **single most important equation** in the chapter.
- [ ] **Half-life:** $t_{1/2} = 0.693 / k$.
- [ ] **Temperature correction:** $k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$, $\theta \approx 1.07$ for hydrocarbons.
- [ ] **Leachate must be collected and treated**; do not let it migrate to groundwater.
- [ ] **VOCs must be captured** via off-gas treatment (biofilter / activated carbon).
- [ ] **Substrate inhibition** occurs at very high TPH (> 50,000 mg/kg) — dilute if needed.
- [ ] **Frequent distinction exam trap:** Prepared beds ≠ Landfarming ≠ Composting ≠ Biopile. Each has unique features.
- [ ] **For numerical problems,** always state assumptions: isothermal, well-mixed, first-order, no mass-transfer limitation.
- [ ] **Units matter:** mg/kg for soil concentration, day⁻¹ for $k$, days for time.
- [ ] **Closed-cell mass balance:** $M_{in} = M_{degraded} + M_{out} + M_{residual}$.
- [ ] **Aeration stoichiometry:** ~ 1.5 mol O₂ per mol organic carbon mineralised (for hydrocarbon oxidation to CO₂).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT754 - BIOREMEDIATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Biopiles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Biopiles

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **biopile** (also called a **bio-cell**, **composting pile**, or **biowindow** in some literature) is an *ex-situ* bioremediation technique in which excavated contaminated soil is formed into engineered piles or windrows and aerated, moistened, and amended with nutrients, bulking agents, and sometimes microbial inocula to stimulate the **microbial degradation** of organic and certain inorganic contaminants.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim alignment):**
> *Biopiles are engineered, controlled, aerobic above-ground treatment systems used for the remediation of contaminated soils. The system optimizes biological activity through the management of oxygen, moisture, temperature, and nutrient supply within a piled soil matrix.*

### Classification of Biopile Systems

| Type | Aeration Mechanism | Typical Contaminants Treated |
|------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| **Static Biopile** | Passive diffusion / vacuum/positive pressure through embedded piping | Petroleum hydrocarbons, PAHs |
| **Aerated Biopile** | Active injection of air via blower + perforated pipes | Diesel, lube oil, creosote |
| **Hood Biopile** | Enclosed hood with off-gas treatment and controlled air flow | Volatile/semi-volatile organics |
| **Window Biopile** | Periodic mechanical turning using a front-end loader | Light to medium hydrocarbon contamination |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Breathing Compost Garden"

Imagine taking a heavily oil-stained patch of soil from a petrol pump site. Instead of dumping it in a landfill, you mound it into a carefully engineered hill, much like a farmer's compost heap. Inside this mound:

- You plant invisible "workers" — bacteria and fungi — by adding nutrients (their food).
- You keep the pile "breathing" by pushing fresh air through buried pipes (oxygen = mandatory for aerobic microbes).
- You sprinkle water so the soil moisture stays in a comfortable range (think of it as keeping the workers hydrated).
- You occasionally stir the pile (turning) so that every grain of soil gets its share of food, water, and oxygen.

Over weeks to months, the "workers" eat the oil for breakfast, lunch, and dinner — converting toxic hydrocarbons into harmless **carbon dioxide**, **water**, and **microbial biomass**. What remains is clean soil, ready to be returned to the land.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A biopile is essentially **soil composting applied to environmental remediation** — controlled, accelerated, and scientifically monitored.

### Key Performance Metrics Highlighted

- **Target contaminant reduction:** Typically **> 70%** within 6–24 months.
- **Soil bed temperature optimum:** **30 °C – 45 °C** (mesophilic range; thermophilic possible up to **55 °C**).
- **Soil moisture content:** Held between **40% – 80% of Water Holding Capacity (WHC)**.
- **Oxygen concentration in off-gas:** Must remain **> 10% (v/v)** to maintain aerobic conditions.
- **C:N:P ratio adjusted to approximately:** **100 : 10 : 1** (microbial stoichiometric optimum).
- **pH range:** **6.0 – 8.0** (neutral to slightly alkaline).
- **Bulk density of pile:** **< 1.4 g/cm³** to ensure adequate porosity and diffusion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Biopiles Matter for KTU Examiners:**
> Biopiles are a favourite KTU module question because they neatly combine *microbial kinetics*, *mass balance*, *soil physics*, and *engineering design* — testing CO2 (Design/Develop solutions) and CO3 (Modern tool usage/environmental engineering) in one go.

---

## 1.3 Where Biopiles Fit in the Remediation Hierarchy

```
Source → Containment → Excavation → BIOPILES (ex-situ, biological) → Clean Soil
                                   ↑
                          Considered a GREEN technology
                          (low energy, low chemical input)
```

Biopiles are placed in the **ex-situ** bioremediation family alongside **landfarming** and **bioreactors**, but biopiles are distinct because:
1. They are **engineered piles (mounded geometry)**, not spread-out fields like landfarming.
2. They incorporate **active aeration** infrastructure.
3. They are typically **enclosed or covered**, allowing leachate and off-gas capture.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — How a Biopile Works (The 5 Pillars)

A biopile succeeds only when **five interdependent parameters** are simultaneously controlled. Loss of control on any one parameter collapses the degradation rate.

### Pillar 1 — Oxygen Delivery (Aeration)

Microbes oxidize contaminants aerobically:
$$ \text{Contaminant} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Microbes}} \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Biomass} $$

**Engineering methods for aeration:**
- **Passive:** Windrows are turned periodically using a mechanical turner.
- **Active:** Air blowers connected to a network of **perforated HDPE/PVC pipes** embedded at the base.
- **Hybrid:** Combination of periodic turning + low-pressure aeration.

### Pillar 2 — Moisture Control

Water is essential for microbial metabolism and for dissolving hydrophobic contaminants (making them bioavailable). Moisture is controlled by:
- Irrigation sprays on top of pile.
- Moisture sensors (TDR probes) inserted at multiple depths.
- Covering pile with breathable geotextile to reduce evaporation.

**Target: 40% – 80% of Water Holding Capacity (WHC).**

### Pillar 3 — Nutrient Amendment (N, P, K, Trace Elements)

Microbes require macronutrients in the ratio of approximately **C : N : P : S = 100 : 10 : 1 : 1**.
Hydrocarbon-contaminated soils are typically **carbon-rich but N/P-deficient**. Therefore, **fertilizers** (urea, ammonium nitrate, triple superphosphate) are added.

### Pillar 4 — Temperature Regulation

- **Optimum:** **30 °C – 35 °C** (mesophilic).
- Below 5 °C → microbial activity ceases.
- Above 55 °C → microbial enzymes denature (thermophilic window exists briefly).
- Pile geometry (height typically 2–4 m) and surface area govern thermal mass.

### Pillar 5 — pH and Contaminant Bioavailability

- Maintain **pH 6.0 – 8.0** using lime (CaCO₃) or sulfur amendments.
- Add **surfactants** (e.g., Tween-80, biosurfactants) to solubilize hydrophobic PAHs and heavy hydrocarbons.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they directly appear in KTU Part B numericals.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use | Standard Units |
|---|----------------------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ | First-order contaminant decay in biopile | $C$ in mg/kg, $t$ in days, $k$ in day$^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $\ln\left(\dfrac{C_t}{C_0}\right) = -kt$ | Linearized form for plot of $\ln(C_t)$ vs $t$ | — |
| 3 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$ | Half-life of contaminant | days |
| 4 | $\mu = \mu_{\max}\dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | Monod microbial growth kinetics | $\mu$ in h$^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $r_{sub} = -\dfrac{\mu_{\max}}{Y} \cdot \dfrac{S \cdot X}{K_s + S}$ | Substrate utilization rate (Monod-based) | mg substrate / (L·h) |
| 6 | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | Specific growth rate definition | h$^{-1}$ |
| 7 | $Y = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | Biomass yield coefficient (mass cells/mass substrate) | dimensionless (g/g) |
| 8 | $WHC = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{dry\,soil}} \times 100$ | Water Holding Capacity calculation | % |
| 9 | $O_2\,demand = 1.5 \times C_{oil} + 4.0 \times C_{phenol}$ | Stoichiometric oxygen demand for hydrocarbon oxidation (theoretical) | kg O₂ / kg contaminant |
| 10 | $\text{Aeration Rate} = \dfrac{Q_{air}}{V_{pile}}$ | Specific air flow requirement | m³ air / (m³ pile·h) |
| 11 | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m_{soil}}{V_{pile}}$ | Bulk density of biopile | g/cm³ or kg/m³ |
| 12 | $\text{Porosity},\ n = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_s}$ | Air-filled porosity available for gas diffusion | dimensionless |
| 13 | $D_{eff} = D_0 \cdot n^{1.5}$ | Effective diffusion coefficient in soil (Millington-Quirk) | cm²/s |
| 14 | $\text{Removal Efficiency},\ \eta = \dfrac{C_0 - C_t}{C_0}\times 100$ | Performance metric | % |

> **Note on stoichiometric oxygen demand (Equation 9):**
> For typical petroleum hydrocarbons with empirical formula ~CH₂, complete oxidation requires ~**3.5 kg O₂ per kg hydrocarbon**. For phenols and PAHs, this rises to ~**4 kg O₂ / kg**.

---

## 2.3 Why Biopiles Work — The Underlying Microbiology

The active degraders in a biopile are largely:
- **Hydrocarbon-degrading bacteria:** *Pseudomonas*, *Rhodococcus*, *Acinetobacter*, *Mycobacterium*.
- **PAH-degraders:** *Sphingomonas*, *Burkholderia*.
- **Fungi (ligninolytic):** *Phanerochaete chrysosporium*, *Trametes versicolor* (especially for high-molecular-weight PAHs).
- **Consortia:** Field soils naturally develop synergistic consortia over time.

The **co-metabolic** and **sequential metabolic** pathways operate: lighter fractions (n-alkanes) are consumed first, paving the way for slower attacks on branched alkanes, aromatics, and PAHs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Biopiles have been successfully deployed at **petrol station sites, refinery yards, wood-preserving facilities (creosote), and military firing ranges (TNT, RDX)** worldwide. They are particularly attractive where:
> - The contaminated zone is **shallow (< 5 m depth)**.
> - **Excavation is feasible** (no buried utilities).
> - **Land is available** adjacent to the site.
> - Climate allows **outdoor operation** (or treatment can be enclosed under a greenhouse/hoop structure).

---

## 2.4 Biopile Construction Sequence

1. **Site preparation** — impermeable HDPE liner (1.5–2.0 mm) + leachate collection trench.
2. **Soil placement** — contaminated soil placed in lifts of 30–50 cm; pile height 2–4 m.
3. **Amendment incorporation** — bulking agent (wood chips, straw, sand) + N/P fertilizer + lime.
4. **Aeration pipe installation** — perforated pipes laid at base in grid pattern.
5. **Irrigation system** — drip lines / sprinklers.
6. **Cover installation** — geotextile, peat layer, or impermeable membrane depending on VOC content.
7. **Monitoring instrumentation** — temperature probes, O₂ probes, moisture sensors, gas sampling ports.
8. **Operation & maintenance** — turning schedule (weekly to monthly) + irrigation + monitoring.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Order Degradation Kinetics in a Biopile

The microbial degradation of most petroleum hydrocarbons in soils follows **first-order kinetics** with respect to the contaminant concentration. This is one of the **most frequently tested derivations** in KTU module examinations.

### Starting Assumption

The rate of contaminant disappearance is proportional to its instantaneous concentration:

$$ r = -\dfrac{dC}{dt} = k \cdot C $$

where:
- $r$ = rate of contaminant degradation (mg/kg·day)
- $C$ = contaminant concentration at time $t$ (mg/kg)
- $k$ = first-order rate constant (day⁻¹)
- $t$ = elapsed time (days)

### Step 1 — Separation of Variables

Bring all $C$-terms to one side and all $t$-terms to the other:

$$ \dfrac{dC}{C} = -k \, dt $$

### Step 2 — Integration

Integrate both sides between the limits $C_0$ at $t = 0$ and $C_t$ at time $t$:

$$ \int_{C_0}^{C_t} \dfrac{dC}{C} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt $$

$$ \left[\ln C\right]_{C_0}^{C_t} = -k \left[t\right]_0^t $$

$$ \ln C_t - \ln C_0 = -kt $$

### Step 3 — Exponential Form

$$ \ln\left(\dfrac{C_t}{C_0}\right) = -kt $$

$$ C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt} $$

This is the **canonical first-order decay equation** universally used in biopile design.

### Step 4 — Half-Life Derivation

At $t = t_{1/2}$, $C_t = C_0 / 2$. Substitute:

$$ \ln\left(\dfrac{C_0/2}{C_0}\right) = -k \cdot t_{1/2} $$

$$ \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -k \cdot t_{1/2} $$

$$ -\ln 2 = -k \cdot t_{1/2} $$

$$ \boxed{t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}} $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Monod Growth Kinetics Linked to Substrate Removal

The Monod equation relates microbial growth rate to substrate (contaminant) concentration.

### Step 1 — Define Specific Growth Rate

By definition:

$$ \mu = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dX}{dt} $$

where $X$ = biomass concentration (mg cells / kg soil).

### Step 2 — Empirical Monod Form

Experimentally, $\mu$ depends hyperbolically on substrate concentration $S$:

$$ \mu = \mu_{\max}\dfrac{S}{K_s + S} $$

where:
- $\mu_{\max}$ = maximum specific growth rate (h⁻¹)
- $K_s$ = half-saturation constant (mg/L)
- $S$ = substrate concentration (mg/L)

### Step 3 — Combine with Mass Balance on Biomass

$$ \dfrac{dX}{dt} = \mu \cdot X = \mu_{\max}\dfrac{S}{K_s + S} \cdot X $$

### Step 4 — Link to Substrate Removal via Yield Coefficient

By definition of yield coefficient $Y$:

$$ Y = -\dfrac{dX}{dS} \Rightarrow dX = -Y \cdot dS $$

Therefore:

$$ \dfrac{dX}{dt} = -Y \cdot \dfrac{dS}{dt} $$

Equating both expressions for $\dfrac{dX}{dt}$:

$$ -Y \cdot \dfrac{dS}{dt} = \mu_{\max}\dfrac{S}{K_s + S} \cdot X $$

$$ \boxed{\dfrac{dS}{dt} = -\dfrac{\mu_{\max}}{Y} \cdot \dfrac{S \cdot X}{K_s + S}} $$

This is the **substrate utilization rate** in a biopile, assuming Monod kinetics dominate. When $S \gg K_s$, the expression collapses to **zero-order**; when $S \ll K_s$, it becomes **first-order** in $S$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Oxygen Demand Stoichiometry for Hydrocarbon Oxidation

A typical diesel-range hydrocarbon (C₁₂H₂₆, dodecane) is oxidized by microbes as:

$$ \text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{26} + a\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow b\,\text{CO}_2 + c\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

### Step 1 — Carbon Balance

12 C atoms on left → $b = 12$ CO₂ produced.

### Step 2 — Hydrogen Balance

26 H atoms → $2c = 26$ → $c = 13$ H₂O produced.

### Step 3 — Oxygen Balance

$2a = 2b + c = 2(12) + 13 = 37$

$$ a = \dfrac{37}{2} = 18.5 $$

So:

$$ \text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{26} + 18.5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 12\,\text{CO}_2 + 13\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

### Step 4 — Convert to Mass Basis

- Molar mass of C₁₂H₂₆ = **170 g/mol**
- Molar mass of O₂ = **32 g/mol**

Mass of O₂ required per kg of hydrocarbon:

$$ \dfrac{18.5 \times 32}{170} = \dfrac{592}{170} \approx 3.48 \text{ kg O}_2 / \text{kg hydrocarbon} $$

**This confirms the rule of thumb: ~3.5 kg O₂ is required to fully oxidize 1 kg of petroleum hydrocarbon.** For PAHs (e.g., naphthalene C₁₀H₈), the figure rises to ~3.85; for phenols ~4.0.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Question Setup)

**Problem Statement:**
A biopile contains **10,000 kg** of soil contaminated with **2,000 mg/kg of total petroleum hydrocarbons (TPH)**. The first-order degradation rate constant is **k = 0.012 day⁻¹**. Determine:

1. The concentration after **90 days**.
2. The time required to reach **500 mg/kg (cleanup target)**.
3. The half-life of TPH.
4. The total oxygen required for full oxidation.
5. The expected CO₂ produced (assuming full mineralization).

### Solution

**Part 1: Concentration after 90 days**

$$ C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt} = 2000 \cdot e^{-0.012 \times 90} $$

$$ C_t = 2000 \cdot e^{-1.08} $$

$$ C_t = 2000 \times 0.3396 $$

$$ C_t \approx 679.3 \text{ mg/kg} $$

**Part 2: Time to reach 500 mg/kg**

$$ \ln\left(\dfrac{500}{2000}\right) = -0.012 \cdot t $$

$$ \ln(0.25) = -0.012 \cdot t $$

$$ -1.3863 = -0.012 \cdot t $$

$$ t = \dfrac{1.3863}{0.012} \approx 115.5 \text{ days} $$

**Part 3: Half-life**

$$ t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{0.012} \approx 57.75 \text{ days} $$

**Part 4: Total O₂ Required**

Total mass of TPH in pile = $10{,}000 \times \dfrac{2000}{10^6}$ = **20 kg TPH**

O₂ demand = $20 \times 3.5 = $ **70 kg O₂**

**Part 5: CO₂ Produced**

For each kg C in hydrocarbon, ~3.67 kg CO₂ is generated (since 12 g C → 44 g CO₂).
Assuming TPH has ~85% C content: Carbon mass = $20 \times 0.85 = 17$ kg.

$$ \text{CO}_2\text{ produced} = 17 \times \dfrac{44}{12} \approx 62.3 \text{ kg CO}_2 $$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Biopile Performance Drivers

| Driver | Too Low Effect | Too High Effect | Engineering Control |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Oxygen | Anaerobic, slow, odorous | Drying, channeling | Blower + turning |
| Moisture | Microbial dormancy | Anaerobic pockets, leachate | Irrigation + cover |
| Temperature (< 10 °C) | Halted activity | Enzyme denaturation (> 55 °C) | Insulation / greenhouse |
| pH (< 5.5) | Inhibited metabolism | Ammonia volatilization (> 9) | Lime / sulfur amendment |
| Nutrient N/P | C-limited, slow | Eutrophication of leachate | Fertilizer dosing |
| Bulking agent | Compaction, low porosity | Reduced soil contact | Wood chips/sand |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked *"Why does a biopile outperform natural attenuation?"* — the answer is *active control of the 5 drivers above*. This is a **7-mark question** magnet.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a biopile. List any four critical operational parameters that must be controlled for its successful performance.**

**Model Answer:**

A **biopile** is an *ex-situ* bioremediation system in which excavated contaminated soil is mounded into engineered piles (typically 2–4 m high) on an impermeable base, and treated by maintaining optimal conditions for **aerobic microbial degradation** of contaminants.

The four critical operational parameters are:
1. **Oxygen supply** (≥ 10% O₂ in off-gas).
2. **Moisture content** (40–80% of Water Holding Capacity).
3. **Temperature** (optimum 30–35 °C).
4. **Nutrient balance** (C:N:P ≈ 100:10:1).
*(Optional 5th — pH between 6.0–8.0)*

**(3 marks)** ✅

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Compare biopiles and landfarming as ex-situ bioremediation techniques (any four points).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Biopile | Landfarming |
|-----------|---------|-------------|
| Pile geometry | Mounded pile 2–4 m tall | Spread in thin layer 0.3–0.5 m |
| Aeration | Active via blowers + turning | Primarily by tilling |
| Leachate control | Yes (lined base) | Minimal |
| VOC/off-gas capture | Possible (enclosed hood) | Not feasible |
| Footprint | Smaller | Larger |
| Capital cost | Higher | Lower |

**(3 marks)** ✅

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled diagram, describe the **constructional features of an engineered biopile**. Discuss the role of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biopile is loaded with soil containing **5,000 mg/kg of diesel-range hydrocarbons**. The first-order degradation rate constant is determined experimentally to be **k = 0.008 day⁻¹**. Calculate:
  - (i) The concentration after **180 days**.
  - (ii) The time required to achieve **90% removal**.
  - (iii) The half-life of the contaminant.
  - (iv) Comment on the suitability of the cleanup time for field deployment. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer for Question A

**(a) Constructional Features of a Biopile (7 marks)**

A biopile consists of the following components from bottom to top:

1. **Impermeable Base Liner** (HDPE, 1.5–2 mm) — Prevents leachate migration into groundwater and provides a clean working surface.
2. **Leachate Collection Layer** (gravel, 10 cm) — Collects and channels percolating water to a sump for treatment.
3. **Aeration Pipe Network** (perforated HDPE pipes in grid) — Distributes air uniformly throughout the pile; ensures aerobic conditions.
4. **Irrigation System** (drip/sprinkler) — Maintains moisture between 40–80% WHC.
