- **Hollow-fiber membrane cassettes** (PVDF material, 0.04 µm pore size, fiber OD ≈ 2 mm) immersed directly in the aerobic zone.
- **Fine-bubble diffusers** on the tank floor supplying both process oxygen and membrane scouring air.
- **Permeate suction pump** providing TMP of **0.2 – 0.4 bar**.
- **Backwash system** using permeate + compressed air at periodic intervals.
- **CIP system** with **NaOCl (500 – 2000 mg/L)** and **citric acid (1000 – 2000 mg/L)** for chemical cleaning.

**2. Working (2 marks):**
- Raw wastewater enters the anoxic zone for denitrification, then flows into the aerobic zone where heterotrophic bacteria oxidize organic pollutants (COD/BOD) into CO₂ and biomass.
- Treated mixed liquor contacts the outer surface of the hollow fibers; clean permeate is drawn inward by suction.
- All biomass, suspended solids, and macromolecules are retained; permeate exits with TSS < 1 mg/L.

**3. Role of Aeration in Fouling Control (2 marks):**
- **Dual function:** Process oxygen + membrane surface scouring.
- Rising air bubbles create a **two-phase cross-flow** along the membrane surface, generating shear stress that continuously disrupts the cake layer.
- Specific aeration demand **SADm = 0.3 – 0.6 m³ air/m³ permeate** is recommended for sustainable operation.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks (Numerical Design):

**Given Data:**
- $Q = 800 \ \text{m}^3/\text{d}$
- $S_0 = 1200 \ \text{mg/L}$
- $J = 18 \ \text{LMH}$
- $F/M = 0.12 \ \text{d}^{-1}$
- $X = 10{,}000 \ \text{mg/L}$

**Step 1: Compute Membrane Area (3 marks)**

$$A_m = \dfrac{1000 \times Q}{24 \times J} = \dfrac{1000 \times 800}{24 \times 18}$$

$$A_m = \dfrac{800{,}000}{432} = 1851.85 \ \text{m}^2$$

Including a 10 % safety margin:

$$A_{design} = 1851.85 \times 1.10 = 2037.04 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute Biological Tank Volume (2 marks)**

$$V_b = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{X \cdot (F/M)} = \dfrac{800 \times 10^3 \times 1200}{10{,}000 \times 0.12 \times 10^3}$$

$$V_b = \dfrac{9.6 \times 10^8}{1.2 \times 10^6} = 800 \ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 3: Compute HRT (2 marks)**

$$HRT = \dfrac{V_b}{Q} = \dfrac{800}{800} = 1 \ \text{day} = 24 \ \text{hours}$$

> [!TIP]
> All values lie within KTU-accepted BMR operating ranges, confirming the design is feasible.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Fouling Analysis & Membrane Selection

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**Explain the Resistance-in-Series model for membrane fouling in a biomembrane reactor. Discuss the typical contributions of $R_m$, $R_c$, $R_p$, and $R_f$ and the mitigation strategies for each.**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**Compare polymeric and ceramic membranes for biomembrane reactor construction. Justify which is preferred for bioremediation of oily petroleum wastewater containing polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs).**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks (Resistance-in-Series Model):

**1. Foundation (3 marks):**
The model extends Darcy's law to a fouled membrane:

$$J = \dfrac{TMP}{\mu \cdot R_t}$$

where total resistance is decomposed as:

$$R_t = R_m + R_c + R_p + R_f$$

**2. Resistance Components (2 marks):**
- **$R_m$ (Intrinsic Membrane Resistance):** Depends on pore size, porosity, and thickness. Measured using clean water before operation. Mitigation: proper material selection (e.g., 0.04 µm PVDF UF).
- **$R_c$ (Cake Layer Resistance):** Forms due to biomass deposition; reversible. Mitigation: continuous air scouring + intermittent backwash.
- **$R_p$ (Pore Blocking):** Small particles lodge inside pores. Mitigation: backwash with permeate + air.
- **$R_f$ (Irreversible Fouling):** Caused by biofilm and adsorbed organic matter. Mitigation: CIP with NaOCl + citric acid.

**3. Typical Contributions (2 marks):**
For a submerged MBR operating at TMP = 0.3 bar and J = 15 LMH, the typical breakdown is:

| Resistance | % Contribution | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| $R_m$ | ~70 % | Material selection |
| $R_c$ | ~20 % | Air scouring |
| $R_p$ | ~7 % | Backwash |
| $R_f$ | ~3 % | CIP cleaning |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks (Membrane Material Comparison):

**1. Polymeric Membranes (2 marks):**
- Examples: PVDF, PES, PP.
- Advantages: Low cost (₹3,000 – 8,000/m²), easy fabrication into hollow fibers, good chemical resistance (especially PVDF).
- Disadvantages: Limited lifespan (5 – 8 years), hydrophobic fouling, low tolerance to aggressive solvents.

**2. Ceramic Membranes (2 marks):**
- Examples: Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, TiO₂.
- Advantages: Exceptional lifespan (15 – 20 years), high thermal and chemical stability, hydrophilic surface (less fouling), withstands pH 0 – 14.
- Disadvantages: Capital cost 5–10× higher, brittle, heavy.

**3. Justification for Oily PAH-Containing Wastewater (3 marks):**

**Ceramic membranes (specifically Al₂O₃ tubular UF) are preferred** because:

- **Hydrophilic surface** resists oil adhesion — polymeric membranes are hydrophobic and foul rapidly.
- **Tubular geometry** accommodates viscous oily fluids with cross-flow velocities up to 4 m/s.
- **Chemical robustness** allows aggressive CIP using hot NaOH (pH 13) and organic solvents for PAH removal.
- **Thermal tolerance** permits steam sterilization between batches, critical for petrochemical applications.
- Although capital cost is high, the **20-year operational lifespan and minimal fouling downtime** justify the investment for hydrocarbon bioremediation.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on Biomembrane Reactor Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — Always state LMH, m², m³/d explicitly. A numerically correct answer without units will be penalized **0.5 – 1 mark**.
> 2. **Conflating HRT and SRT** — KTU examiners *specifically* test whether you understand that in a BMR, HRT ≠ SRT and that they can be independently controlled. Confusing them = **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Not mentioning membrane material** — A 14-mark design question without specifying PVDF, PES, or ceramic is incomplete. Always state material AND pore size.
> 4. **Ignoring the role of aeration** — Many students describe fouling but omit the *mechanism* by which air scouring controls cake formation. This loses **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Mixing up submerged vs. side-stream configurations** — Be explicit: submerged = vacuum, side-stream = pressure. Examiners notice this immediately.
> 6. **Skipping the safety factor** — Always apply a **10 – 15 % design safety margin** to the membrane area to account for long-term fouling.
> 7. **Not drawing or describing the schematic** — Even in numerical parts, a brief flow description is expected.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Construction of Biomembrane Reactors:**

### 🔹 Core Definitions
- **Biomembrane Reactor (BMR / MBR)** = Biological degradation + Membrane separation.
- **Permeate** = Clean water passing through membrane.
- **Retentate/Concentrate** = Biomass-rich stream recycled to the bioreactor.

### 🔹 Membrane Geometry
- **Hollow Fiber** — highest packing density (5000–30000 m²/m³).
- **Flat Sheet** — modular and easy to replace.
- **Tubular** — best for viscous/oily waste.
- **Spiral Wound** — high flux but fouling-prone.

### 🔹 Membrane Materials
- **Polymeric:** PVDF (most common), PES (high temp), PP (anaerobic).
- **Ceramic:** Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, TiO₂ — premium, durable, hydrophilic.

### 🔹 Operating Parameters (Must Memorize)
- TMP = **0.1 – 0.5 bar** (submerged), **1 – 4 bar** (side-stream).
- Flux = **10 – 50 LMH**.
- MLSS = **8,000 – 15,000 mg/L** (3–4× CAS).
- HRT = **2 – 24 h**, SRT = **20 – 60 d** (decoupled).
- F/M = **0.05 – 0.2 d⁻¹**.

### 🔹 Critical Equations (Score Boosters)
- $J = \dfrac{Q_p}{A_m}$
- $J = \dfrac{TMP}{\mu \cdot R_t}$
- $R_t = R_m + R_c + R_p + R_f$
- $A_m = \dfrac{1000 \cdot Q}{24 \cdot J}$
- $V_b = \dfrac{Q \cdot S_0}{X \cdot (F/M)}$
- $HRT = \dfrac{V_b}{Q}$

### 🔹 Fouling Control Hierarchy
- **1st line:** Continuous coarse-bubble air scouring.
- **2nd line:** Periodic backwash (every 8 – 30 min).
- **3rd line:** Intermittent relaxation cycles.
- **4th line:** CIP (every 3 – 6 months).

### 🔹 Default Answer Templates for KTU
- "Design for municipal wastewater" → **Submerged, hollow fiber, PVDF, UF (0.04 µm)**.
- "Design for oily/viscous industrial wastewater" → **Side-stream, tubular, ceramic (Al₂O₃), UF**.
- "Pharma/micropollutant removal" → **Submerged UF + NF/RO polishing**.
- "Heavy metals" → **Anaerobic BMR + chemical precipitation or RO**.

### 🔹 Top Three Advantages of BMRs (Always Cite These)
1. Complete biomass retention → high MLSS → smaller tank volume.
2. Decoupled HRT and SRT → operational flexibility.
3. Effluent TSS < 1 mg/L → near-reuse quality.

### 🔹 Top Three Limitations (For Balanced Answers)
1. Membrane fouling → frequent cleaning.
2. High capital cost (membrane replacement every 5–8 years for polymeric).
3. Energy for aeration and pumping.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biodegradation of Polyhalogenated Compounds by Genetically Engineered Bacteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT754 - BIOREMEDIATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biodegradation of Polyhalogenated Compounds by Genetically Engineered Bacteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Polyhalogenated Compounds (PHCs)** are xenobiotic organic molecules that contain two or more covalently bonded halogen atoms (fluorine, chlorine, bromine, or iodine) attached to a carbon backbone. The carbon–halogen (C–X) bond is among the strongest single bonds in organic chemistry, with bond dissociation energies ranging from **≈ 327 kJ/mol (C–I)** to **≈ 485 kJ/mol (C–F)**, which is the primary reason these compounds resist natural degradation and persist in the environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Polyhalogenated compounds are organochlorine, organobromine, and organofluorine xenobiotics that accumulate in biological systems due to their lipophilicity, chemical stability, and resistance to attack by conventional hydrolytic/oxidative enzymes. They include Polychlorinated Biphenyls (PCBs), Polychlorinated Dibenzo-p-dioxins (PCDDs), Dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane (DDT), Hexachlorocyclohexane (HCH/Lindane), and Trichloroethylene (TCE).*

**Genetically Engineered Bacteria (GEB)** — also called **Genetically Engineered Microorganisms (GEMs)** or **Recombinant Bioremediation Agents** — are microbial strains whose catabolic genome has been intentionally modified using **recombinant DNA technology (rDNA)**, **genome editing (CRISPR-Cas)**, or **directed evolution** to express novel or enhanced enzymatic pathways capable of mineralising recalcitrant halogenated pollutants.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Toxic Waste Specialist"

Imagine a team of city sanitation workers. Their normal job is to collect household garbage (simple organics like sugars, fats). One day, a truckload of **industrial chemical drums** is dumped in the city. The regular workers don't recognise the labels, don't have the right tools, and some drums leak toxic fumes that make them sick.

- **Native microbes** = Regular sanitation workers → They can slowly and inefficiently deal with a few industrial drums, but most of the chemical waste just sits there for years.
- **Genetically engineered bacteria** = Specially trained "HAZMAT specialists" → We take a regular worker, send them to a custom training academy (insert genes), and equip them with:
  - 🛠️ **New tools** (novel enzymes like dehalogenases)
  - 🧠 **Better instructions** (operons/pathways for catabolism)
  - 🛡️ **Protective gear** (stress-response proteins for solvent/tolerance)

The result: a microbe that can **break down, transform, or completely mineralise** the stubborn toxic drum into harmless end-products like CO₂, H₂O, and chloride ions (Cl⁻).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Mapping (KTU Board Standard):**
> - **Mineralisation** → Complete conversion to inorganic end-products (CO₂ + H₂O + HCl)
> - **Detoxification** → Conversion to a less toxic intermediate
> - **Co-metabolism** → Transformation by enzymes whose primary substrate is different from the pollutant
> - **Reductive dehalogenation** → Halogen removal by replacement with hydrogen (anaerobic)

### 1.3 Why Natural Degradation Fails — The Need for Engineering

| Natural Limitation | Engineering Solution |
|---|---|
| Slow growth on chlorinated substrates | Insert **regulatory switches** to express pathways constitutively |
| Toxicity of intermediates kills the microbe | Add **efflux pumps** and **stress-tolerance genes** |
| Narrow substrate specificity | Engineer **broad-specificity dehalogenases** via site-directed mutagenesis |
| Incomplete degradation | Assemble **hybrid pathways** (upper + lower from different organisms) |
| Anaerobic requirement for PCBs | Transfer anaerobic dehalogenases into **aerobic hosts** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Biochemical Mechanisms of Dehalogenation

The C–X bond can be cleaved by **four primary biochemical strategies**. KTU examiners frequently test the mechanistic distinction between these.

#### (A) Reductive Dehalogenation
- Replacement of halogen (X) with hydrogen (H)
- Dominant in **anaerobic** conditions
- Catalysed by **corrinoid/Fe-S coreductases** (e.g., tetrachloroethene reductive dehalogenase, **PceA**)
- Example: perchloroethylene (PCE) → trichloroethylene (TCE) → cis-dichloroethylene (cis-DCE) → vinyl chloride → ethene

$$\text{CCl}_2{=}\text{CCl}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \xrightarrow{\text{PceA}} \text{CCl}_2{=}\text{CHCl} + \text{Cl}^-$$

#### (B) Oxidative Dehalogenation
- **Aerobic** mechanism; oxygen atom inserted via **monooxygenase** or **dioxygenase**
- Generates unstable gem-halohydrins that spontaneously eliminate HX
- Used for chlorinated phenols and PCBs

$$\text{Ar–Cl} + \text{O}_2 + \text{NADH} + \text{H}^+ \xrightarrow{\text{monooxygenase}} \text{Ar–OH} + \text{HCl} + \text{NAD}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

#### (C) Hydrolytic Dehalogenation
- Direct nucleophilic substitution of X by –OH from H₂O
- Catalysed by **haloalkane dehalogenases (DhaA, LinB, DhlA)**
- Most studied enzyme: **LinB** from *Sphingomonas paucimobilis* UT26 for HCH degradation

$$\text{R–CH}_2\text{–Cl} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{haloalkane dehalogenase}} \text{R–CH}_2\text{–OH} + \text{HCl}$$

#### (D) Thiolytic / Glutathione-dependent Dehalogenation
- Conjugation of the halogen-bearing carbon with reduced glutathione (GSH)
- Common in **LinA-mediated** dehydrochlorination of HCH isomers

$$\gamma\text{-HCH} \xrightarrow{\text{LinA (dehydrochlorinase)}} \text{PCCH} + 3\,\text{HCl}$$

### 2.2 Genetic Engineering Strategies for Constructing GEB

**Step 1 — Pathway Cloning:** Isolate catabolic genes (*linA, linB, bph, tod, tfd*) from donor organisms using PCR or genomic library screening.

**Step 2 — Vector Construction:** Assemble the genes into a suitable expression vector:
- **Broad-host-range plasmids** (pBBR1MCS, RSF1010) for Gram-negative hosts
- **Transposons** (Tn5, Tn7) for chromosomal integration stability
- **Synthetic operons** under **tac**, **lac**, or **T7** promoters

**Step 3 — Transformation / Conjugation:** Introduce the construct into the host via:
- Electroporation (most common)
- Triparental mating (conjugation)
- CRISPR-Cas9 mediated chromosomal knock-in

**Step 4 — Regulatory Optimisation:** Eliminate native repression; replace weak promoters with strong, **inducer-independent (constitutive) promoters** like *P<sub>em7</sub>* or *P<sub>lacUV5</sub>*.

**Step 5 — Field Validation:** Test survival, catabolic activity, and **Gene Escape Containment** (suicide genes, auxotrophic markers).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Master Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following master table is a **board-exam revision anchor** — every line is a high-yield point for KTU 2024 ESE.

| Target Pollutant | Chemical Class | Key Native Degrader | Engineered Strain Constructed | Dehalogenase Gene Used | Net Reaction |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| γ-HCH (Lindane) | Organochlorine pesticide | *Sphingomonas paucimobilis* UT26 | *Pseudomonas putida* + UT26 genes | *linA, linB, linC, linD* | C₆H₆Cl₆ → 2-hydroxybenzaldehyde → CO₂ |
| PCBs (Aroclor 1242) | Polychlorinated biphenyls | *Burkholderia xenovorans* LB400 | *P. putida* + *bphABCDEFGHIJK* | *bphA* (dioxygenase) | Biphenyl + O₂ → 2,3-dihydroxybiphenyl |
| DDT | Organochlorine pesticide | *Sphingobacterium* sp. | Recombinant *E. coli* | *linA* variants | DDT → DDD → DDE → DDMU |
| TCE / PCE | Chlorinated ethenes | *Dehalococcoides* sp. | *E. coli* + *pceA, tceA* | *pceA* (reductive) | PCE → TCE → DCE → ethene |
| Atrazine | Chlorinated triazine | *Pseudomonas* sp. ADP | Engineered *P. putida* | *atzA, atzB, atzC* | Atrazine → cyanuric acid → CO₂ |
| 2,4-D | Chlorophenoxy acid | *Ralstonia eutropha* | *P. putida* pJP4 | *tfdA, tfdB* | 2,4-D → 2,4-dichlorophenol → CO₂ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constants (K<sub>ow</sub>, log):** Highly chlorinated PHCs have log K<sub>ow</sub> values between **3 (lindane) and 7 (Aroclor 1260)**. KTU expects students to know that log K<sub>ow</sub> > 3 indicates **bioaccumulation potential**, justifying why **engineered bioremediation** is required.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **EPA Superfund Site Remediation** — GE *Burkholderia* deployed at Hudson River PCBs.
2. **In-situ Bioremediation** of chlorinated-solvent contaminated aquifers using *Dehalococcoides* bioaugmentation.
3. **Zero-Waste Biorefineries** — engineered *Pseudomonas* converts chlorinated lignin-derived aromatics into **PHA bioplastics**.
4. **Industrial Effluent Treatment** — GEMs degrade chlorophenols in paper-mill wastewater (Kraft pulp process).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Engineering of *Pseudomonas putida* KT2440 for PCB Mineralisation

The complete PCB degradation requires the **Upper Biphenyl Pathway (UBP)** and the **Lower Biphenyl Pathway (LBP)**. We will derive the logical construction of a recombinant strain step by step.

**Step 1 — Source of Upper Pathway (bphA-I):**
Donor: *Burkholderia xenovorans* LB400. The *bphA* (large subunit) + *bphA2* (small subunit) + *bphB* (dihydrodiol dehydrogenase) form the **ring-hydroxylating dioxygenase** complex.

**Step 2 — Source of Lower Pathway:**
The meta-cleavage pathway converts 2,3-dihydroxybiphenyl into **2-hydroxy-6-oxo-6-phenylhexa-2,4-dienoate** (HOPDA) and then to **chlorobenzoic acid** (CBA).

**Step 3 — Construction of Recombinant Plasmid:**

The plasmid **pWW0** (from *P. putida* mt-2) carries the TOL pathway, providing both *xylXY* genes that further catabolise CBA via catechol → *cis,cis*-muconate → acetyl-CoA.

**Step 4 — Insertion into host chromosome:**
Tn5 transposon delivery inserts the hybrid operon into the chromosome of *P. putida* KT2440 at a neutral *attB* site to ensure **long-term expression without plasmid loss**.

**Step 5 — Energy-yielding logic of mineralisation:**

For one molecule of 2,2',4,4'-tetrachlorobiphenyl (C₁₂H₆Cl₄):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_6\text{Cl}_4 + 12\,\text{O}_2 &\xrightarrow{\text{bphA, bphB, bphC, bphD}} 4\,\text{CO}_2\,(\text{from one ring}) + \text{chlorinated benzoate} \\
\text{Chlorobenzoate} + 2\,\text{O}_2 &\xrightarrow{\text{tfd, xyl genes}} 7\,\text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{HCl} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \\
\hline
\text{Net: } \text{C}_{12}\text{H}_6\text{Cl}_4 + 14\,\text{O}_2 &\longrightarrow 11\,\text{CO}_2 + 4\,\text{HCl} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}$$

**Explanation of derivation:** The first reaction cleaves the **C–C bond between the two aromatic rings** and opens the structure via *meta* cleavage (2,3-dihydroxybiphenyl-1,2-dioxygenase, *bphC*). This yields one molecule of **chlorinated benzoic acid** plus one molecule of a **linear aliphatic fragment**, the latter being directly funnelled to the TCA cycle. The chlorobenzoate ring is then opened by the *ben* or *xyl* pathway catechol-2,3-dioxygenase. Mass balance of carbon: 12 C → 11 CO₂ + 1 C retained as HCl–CO₂ not formed; the fourth HCl is released stoichiometrically, explaining the **+4 HCl** term.

**Step 6 — Theoretical Gibbs Free Energy Yield:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^{\circ\prime} \text{ of complete combustion} &\approx -5680\,\text{kJ/mol (per biphenyl)} \\
\text{ATP yield via oxidative phosphorylation (assuming } {\sim}\,40\% \text{ P/O)} &\approx 95\,\text{mol ATP / mol PCB}
\end{aligned}$$

This is why *P. putida* grows robustly on PCBs as a **sole carbon source** when properly engineered.

### 3.2 Construction Logic for Lindane-Degrading *Pseudomonas*

The complete γ-HCH pathway requires the *lin* operon, comprising **four sequential genes**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\gamma\text{-HCH} &\xrightarrow{\text{LinA (dehydrochlorinase)}} \text{γ-1,3,4,5,6-pentachlorocyclohexene (PCCH)} + 3\,\text{HCl} \\
\text{PCCH} &\xrightarrow{\text{LinB (haloalkane dehalogenase)}} \text{2,5-dichloro-2,5-cyclohexadiene-1,4-diol (2,5-DDOL)} + 2\,\text{HCl} \\
\text{2,5-DDOL} &\xrightarrow{\text{LinC (NAD-dependent dehydrogenase)}} \text{2,5-dichlorohydroquinone (2,5-DCHQ)} \\
\text{2,5-DCHQ} &\xrightarrow{\text{LinD (reductive dehalogenase)}} \text{2-chlorohydroquinone (2-CHQ)} + \text{HCl} \\
\text{2-CHQ} &\xrightarrow{\text{LinE, LinF, LinG, LinH, LinJ, LinK, LinL}} \beta\text{-ketoadipate} \rightarrow \text{TCA cycle} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Derivation logic:** Each Lin enzyme has a defined substrate-product pair. The construction of a complete GEM requires **cloning of the entire 14-kb lin operon** (linA through linJ) under a single strong promoter into a vector like **pVLT31** and mobilisation into *P. putida* KT2440 via **triparental mating** with helper strain *E. coli* HB101 (pRK600).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix of Engineering Tools

| Genetic Tool | Function | Example for PHC Bioremediation |
|---|---|---|
| **Reporter genes** (GFP, *luxAB*) | Monitor degradation in real-time | *gfp* fused to *bphC* promoter tracks PCB exposure |
| **Suicide cassettes** (*sacB*, *parB*) | Containment of GEMs after field use | *sacB* kills cells lacking sucrose resistance |
| **Biosensor promoters** (*bphR*, *todR*) | Detect and respond to pollutant | *Pseudomonas* PCB biosensor turns on at >1 ppm |
| **Stress-tolerance genes** (*groEL, dps, otsAB*) | Protect against solvent toxicity | *groEL* over-expression tolerates 1% (v/v) toluene |
| **CRISPRi** | Repress competing pathways to redirect flux | Block *ben* pathway to force flux through *bph* |
| **Directed evolution** (error-prone PCR) | Improve enzyme kinetics | LinA mutants degrade α-HCH 40× faster |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stoichiometric Clue (KTU Pitfall):** Each HCH molecule releases **4 HCl** upon full mineralisation. At pH < 4, the resulting acidification **inhibits bacterial metabolism**. Practical bioreactor designs therefore include **buffering (NaHCO₃) or pH-stat systems** — this is a common KTU 14-mark question.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define genetically engineered bacteria (GEMs) for bioremediation. List any two advantages of using GEMs over native microorganisms for the degradation of polyhalogenated compounds.*

**Model Answer:**
**Genetically Engineered Microorganisms (GEMs)** are microbial strains whose genome has been deliberately modified using recombinant DNA technology to express novel catabolic activities, especially for the breakdown of recalcitrant xenobiotics.

**Advantages (any two):**
1. **Enhanced substrate range** — pathway engineering combines upper and lower degradative operons from multiple organisms, enabling breakdown of pollutants that no single native microbe can mineralise.
2. **Higher degradation efficiency** — expression under strong constitutive promoters removes native repression, increasing the rate of pollutant turnover.
3. **Improved tolerance to toxic intermediates** — incorporation of stress-response genes (e.g., *groEL*, efflux pumps) prevents self-intoxication.
4. **Controllable containment** — suicide cassettes (e.g., *sacB*) ensure biosafe termination after field application.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*With a suitable example, explain the mechanism of reductive dehalogenation in polyhalogenated compound degradation.*

**Model Answer:**
**Reductive dehalogenation** is an anaerobic mechanism in which the halogen atom is replaced by a hydrogen atom, with the halogen released as a halide ion (X⁻). The reaction requires an electron donor (commonly H₂ or formate) and is catalysed by **corrinoid/iron-sulphur dehalogenases** such as **PceA** (perchloroethylene reductive dehalogenase) in *Dehalococcoides ethenogenes*.

**Example:** Sequential reductive dechlorination of perchloroethylene (PCE):

$$\text{PCE} \rightarrow \text{TCE} \rightarrow \text{cis-DCE} \rightarrow \text{vinyl chloride} \rightarrow \text{ethene (non-toxic)}$$

The released chloride ions raise the medium's salinity, which must be buffered in practical bioreactors.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split typically as **Part (a) 7 marks + Part (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice between Question A and Question B is provided in the ESE paper. Below, two complete alternative questions are given for full preparation coverage.

---

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *Discuss the various biochemical mechanisms of dehalogenation in polyhalogenated compounds with suitable examples. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Polyhalogenated compound degradation proceeds through **four key biochemical mechanisms**:

1. **Reductive Dehalogenation** — Anaerobic; halogen replaced by H. Enzymes: corrinoid/Fe-S reductases. Example: PCE → TCE → ethene by *Dehalococcoides*.

2. **Oxidative Dehalogenation** — Aerobic; O₂ inserted, gem-halohydrin intermediate spontaneously loses HX. Example: chlorobenzene → catechol via toluene dioxygenase (Tod).

3. **Hydrolytic Dehalogenation** — Water acts as nucleophile. Enzymes: haloalkane dehalogenases (LinB, DhaA). Example: 1-chlorobutane → 1-butanol.

4. **Thiolytic/Glutathione-dependent Dehalogenation** — GSH attacks the C–X carbon, releasing X⁻. Example: LinA-mediated dehydrochlorination of γ-HCH.

**Evaluation key words:** reductive, oxidative, hydrolytic, thiolytic; PceA, LinB, DhaA; anaerobic vs aerobic; halide release.

**(b)** *Describe the construction of a genetically engineered *Pseudomonas putida* for complete mineralisation of PCBs. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

A complete PCB-degrading *P. putida* is constructed as follows:

1. **Gene source** — *bphA-F* (upper pathway) from *Burkholderia xenovorans* LB400; *bphG-J* and *ben* genes from *Comamonas testosteroni*.

2. **Vector design** — Clone into broad-host-range plasmid **pMHR1** under the **tac** promoter; add a **GFP reporter** for monitoring.

3. **Insertion** — Electroporation into *P. putida* KT2440; or chromosomal integration via **Tn7 transposon** for stability.

4. **Regulatory modification** — Replace native repressors; insert *groEL* chaperone gene for solvent tolerance.

5. **Containment** — Add *sacB* suicide cassette to terminate cells post-bioremediation.

6. **Validation** — Grow recombinant on Aroclor-1242-spiked mineral medium; quantify Cl⁻ release and CO₂ evolution.

**Net stoichiometry:** C₁₂H₆Cl₄ + 14 O₂ → 11 CO₂ + 4 HCl + 2 H₂O

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *Explain the lin operon-based degradation of γ-hexachlorocyclohexane (lindane). Draw the pathway and identify the enzymes involved. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

γ-HCH (lindane) is degraded by the *lin* operon originally isolated from *Sphingomonas paucimobilis* UT26. The pathway proceeds through **seven sequential enzymatic steps**:

| Step | Substrate | Enzyme | Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | γ-HCH | **LinA** (dehydrochlorinase) | γ-1,3,4,5,6-PCCH + 3 HCl |
| 2 | PCCH | **LinB** (haloalkane dehalogenase) | 2,5-DDOL + 2 HCl |
| 3 | 2,5-DDOL | **LinC** (dehydrogenase) | 2,5-DCHQ |
| 4 | 2,5-DCHQ | **LinD** (reductive dehalogenase) | 2-CHQ + HCl |
| 5 | 2-CHQ | **LinE, LinF** | γ-hydroxymuconicsemialdehyde |
| 6 | semialdehyde | **LinG, LinH, LinJ** | maleylacetate |
| 7 | maleylacetate | **LinK, LinL** | β-ketoadipate → TCA cycle |

**Mass balance:** γ-HCH (C₆H₆Cl₆) + 6 H₂O + 6 O₂ → 6 CO₂ + 6 HCl + 6 H₂O

**(b)** *Discuss the biosafety concerns and containment strategies associated with the release of GEMs into the environment. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The deliberate release of genetically engineered bacteria raises **four major biosafety concerns**:

1. **Horizontal Gene Transfer (HGT)** — Conjugation can transfer engineered catabolic genes to native microbiota, creating **unintended super-degraders** with unpredictable ecological impact.

2. **Disruption of Microbial Communities** — GEMs may out-compete native species, destabilising nitrogen fixation, methanogenesis, or other critical biogeochemical cycles.

3. **Pathogenicity Risk** — If a pathogenic chassis is used (e.g., *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*), engineered virulence traits could arise.

4. **Persistence and Bioaccumulation** — Engineered organisms may persist longer than intended, leading to uncontrolled spread.

**Containment Strategies:**

- **Biological containment** — *sacB* suicide cassette (kills cells in absence of sucrose) or auxotrophic markers (require thymidine).
- **Physical containment** — Encapsulation in alginate or polyvinyl alcohol beads.
- **Temporal containment** — Engineered cells designed to undergo programmed cell death after pollutant depletion.
- **Monitoring** — GFP/*lux* reporters + qPCR tracking of strain abundance.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never** use the term "PCB-degrading bacteria" without specifying the **strain name and the pathway** (e.g., *B. xenovorans* LB400 / *bph* operon). Generic answers lose 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Always** show the **stoichiometric mass balance** with the net equation for mineralisation — examiners reward balanced equations in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Common confusion:** Students often write "dehalogenases release chlorine gas (Cl₂)" — **wrong**. The product is **chloride ion (Cl⁻)** in aqueous solution, **not** molecular chlorine.
> 4. **Avoid** stating that "GMOs are completely banned" — the correct KTU answer is that they are **regulated and used under strict biosafety frameworks (e.g., EPA TSCA, Indian Rules 1989)**.
> 5. **For anaerobic vs aerobic:** Do not mix the two — reductive dehalogenases do **not** function in oxic conditions due to oxygen sensitivity of the corrinoid cofactor.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Polyhalogenated compounds (PHCs)** are recalcitrant due to strong C–X bonds (327–485 kJ/mol).
- [x] Major PHCs of KTU importance: **PCBs, DDT, γ-HCH, TCE/PCE, 2,4-D, dioxins**.
- [x] **Four dehalogenation mechanisms:** reductive, oxidative, hydrolytic, thiolytic.
- [x] **Key engineered strains:** *P. putida* KT2440, *B. xenovorans* LB400, *E. coli* recombinant, *Dehalococcoides* sp.
- [x] **Key enzymes to memorise:** LinA, LinB, LinC, LinD, BphA, BphC, PceA, TfdA, AtzA.
- [x] **Net equations** for PCB, HCH, and TCE mineralisation are **high-yield**.
- [x] log K<sub>ow</sub> > 3 implies bioaccumulation — engineering is required to address lipophilic persistence.
- [x] **Biosafety containment** uses *sacB* suicide genes, auxotrophy, and reporter tracking.
- [x] **Mass-balance pitfall:** Mineralisation yields Cl⁻, **not** Cl₂.
- [x] **Pathway engineering** = combining *upper* (ring-cleavage) and *lower* (β-ketoadipate) operons from different organisms into a single host.
- [x] **CRISPR-Cas9** is the modern tool for chromosomal integration of catabolic operons.
- [x] **Co-metabolism** = gratuitous transformation without growth support; not the same as mineralisation.
- [x] **HCl release** during PHC degradation acidifies the medium — bioreactor buffering is essential.
- [x] **Constitutive promoters** (e.g., *P<sub>em7</sub>*, *P<sub>lacUV5</sub>*) are preferred over inducible ones for field deployment.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:** Memorise the **six signature net equations** (PCB, γ-HCH, DDT, TCE, 2,4-D, atrazine), the **four mechanisms**, and the **three named engineered strains**. These alone account for ≥ 60% of ESE marks on this topic.
